```mermaid
        V5 --> V6[FUNCTIONAL beta galactosidase Tetramer]
        V6 --> V7[Cleaves X gal into Indigo Blue]
        V7 --> V8[BLUE COLONY Observed]
    end
    subgraph RecombinantVector["CASE 2: Recombinant Vector lacZ alpha Disrupted"]
        R1[Repressor LacI Binds Plac] --> R2[IPTG Added Sequesters LacI]
        R2 --> R3[Transcription of Disrupted lacZ alpha mRNA]
        R3 --> R4[Insert Adds Stop Codon or Causes Frameshift]
        R4 --> R5[Translation of Truncated or Non functional Peptide]
        R5 --> R6[No alpha Peptide or Non functional Fragment]
        R6 --> R7[NO Functional beta galactosidase Forms]
        R7 --> R8[X gal Remains Uncleaved and Colorless]
        R8 --> R9[WHITE COLONY Observed]
    end
    style V8 fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    style V9 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style R8 fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    style R9 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define blue-white screening and name the enzyme and substrates involved in this technique.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Blue-white screening is a phenotypic selection method used in molecular cloning to differentiate recombinant plasmid-containing colonies (white) from non-recombinant ones (blue). It relies on the enzyme **β-galactosidase (encoded by the *lacZα* gene)** and the chromogenic substrate **X-gal (5-bromo-4-chloro-3-indolyl-β-D-galactopyranoside)**, with induction by **IPTG (Isopropyl β-D-1-thiogalactopyranoside)**. Functional β-galactosidase cleaves X-gal to produce an insoluble **indigo blue** pigment; colonies with disrupted *lacZα* (due to insert) remain white. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is α-complementation? State the role of the *E. coli* DH5α host strain.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
α-complementation is the **intramolecular association of the α-peptide (encoded by plasmid *lacZα*) with the ω-fragment (encoded by chromosomal *lacZΔM15*)** in the host cell to form a functional β-galactosidase tetramer. *E. coli* DH5α carries the ***lacZΔM15* mutation**, providing the ω-fragment in *trans*, and is the standard host for blue-white screening. Other beneficial mutations in DH5α include ***recA1*** (prevents recombination) and ***endA1*** (improves plasmid quality). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **principle, procedure, and mechanism** of blue-white screening for the selection of recombinant clones. Include the roles of IPTG, X-gal, and ampicillin in the system.

**(b) [7 marks]** A ligation reaction was set up with pUC19 and a 1.5 kb insert. After transformation into DH5α and plating on LB-Amp-X-gal-IPTG agar, **120 white colonies and 30 blue colonies** were observed. Calculate:
   (i) Total colonies
   (ii) Recombination efficiency (%)
   (iii) If 100 µL of 1 mL outgrowth was plated and 10 ng of plasmid was used, calculate the transformation efficiency in CFU/µg.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Principle (2 marks):** Blue-white screening is based on **insertional inactivation** of the *lacZα* gene located on the plasmid vector. The MCS is engineered *within* *lacZα*, so ligation of foreign DNA at this site disrupts the reading frame, abolishing β-galactosidase production.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. The vector (e.g., pUC19) and the insert are digested with compatible restriction enzymes.
2. The linearized vector is dephosphorylated using **Calf Intestinal Phosphatase (CIP)** to prevent self-ligation.
3. T4 DNA ligase joins the insert to the vector (3:1 insert:vector ratio, 16 °C overnight).
4. The ligation mixture is transformed into competent ***E. coli* DH5α** by heat shock at 42 °C for 45 sec.
5. Cells are plated on **LB-Agar + Ampicillin (100 µg/mL) + X-gal (40 µg/mL) + IPTG (0.1 mM)** and incubated at 37 °C for 16–24 hours.
6. Colonies are scored for color: **blue** = non-recombinant; **white** = putative recombinant.

**Roles of Key Components (2 marks):**
- **Ampicillin (100 µg/mL):** Selects for *ampR*-carrying transformants; non-transformed cells are killed.
- **IPTG (0.1 mM):** Binds LacI repressor → induces *lacZα* transcription.
- **X-gal (40 µg/mL):** Chromogenic substrate cleaved by β-galactosidase to release **indigo blue** color.
- **LB-Agar:** Nutrient-rich solid growth medium.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $N_w = 120$ (white), $N_b = 30$ (blue), $V_{plated} = 100\,\mu L$ out of $V_{total} = 1000\,\mu L$, $m_{plasmid} = 10\,ng = 0.01\,\mu g$

**(i) Total Colonies [2 marks]:**

$$
N_t = N_w + N_b = 120 + 30 = 150 \text{ colonies}
$$

**(ii) Recombination Efficiency [2 marks]:**

$$
\eta_{recomb} = \frac{N_w}{N_t} \times 100\% = \frac{120}{150} \times 100\% = 80\%
$$

**(iii) Transformation Efficiency [3 marks]:**

$$
\eta_{transform} = \frac{N_t \times V_{resuspension}}{m_{plasmid} \times V_{plated}} = \frac{150 \times 1000}{0.01 \times 100}
$$

$$
\eta_{transform} = \frac{150{,}000}{1} = 1.5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{CFU/µg}
$$

This is in the **lower acceptable range (10⁵ – 10⁶ CFU/µg)** — indicating room for improving competent cell preparation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **structure of pUC19 vector** with a neat diagram. Describe the role of each functional element with special emphasis on the Multiple Cloning Site (MCS) and the *lacZα* gene.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of blue-white screening. How are white colonies further confirmed as true recombinants? List the **controls** that should be included in any blue-white screening experiment.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

**pUC19 Vector (2686 bp) — Functional Elements:**

| Element | Function | Position |
|---|---|---|
| **pMB1 origin of replication** | High-copy-number replication (~500–700 copies/cell) | — |
| **AmpR (β-lactamase gene)** | Confers ampicillin resistance for selection | — |
| **MCS (Multiple Cloning Site)** | Contains 13 unique restriction sites for insert cloning | 396–454 bp |
| ***lacZα* gene** | Encodes α-peptide of β-galactosidase; site of insertional inactivation | — |
| **Plac promoter** | IPTG-inducible promoter driving *lacZα* expression | — |
| **CAP binding site** | Enhances transcription from *lac* promoter | — |

**MCS Unique Restriction Sites (5'→3' on sense strand):**
HindIII, SphI, PstI, SalI, HincII, AccI, XbaI, BamHI, SmaI, XmaI, AvaI, KpnI, SacI, EcoRI.

**The *lacZα* gene** contains the MCS *in-frame* with its coding sequence. Cloning into **any MCS site** disrupts α-peptide production, leading to **white colonies** on X-gal/IPTG plates.

**(See Section 4.2 diagram for schematic representation.)**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. **Rapid and visual** — results available within 16–24 hours.
2. **High throughput** — thousands of colonies can be screened simultaneously.
3. **Cost-effective** — only X-gal and IPTG are required; no radioactive or expensive probes.
4. **High reliability** — > 80% accuracy in distinguishing recombinants when dephosphorylation is performed.
5. **Compatible with high-heat methods** — works with DH5α, JM109, XL1-Blue, and TOP10.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Requires *lacZΔM15* host strain** — cannot be used with all *E. coli* strains.
2. **False positives** — small inserts or frame-shifts may still produce partial blue color.
3. **False negatives** — some in-frame insertions may not disrupt the α-peptide.
4. **Not a definitive test** — confirmation by PCR, restriction digestion, or sequencing is mandatory.

**Confirmation Methods (1 mark):**
- **Colony PCR** with insert-specific primers.
- **Plasmid miniprep + restriction digestion** to release insert.
- **Sanger sequencing** for absolute validation.

**Essential Controls (1 mark):**
- **Positive control:** uncut vector (should give no colonies on Amp).
- **Negative control:** linearized vector without ligase (background colonies from intact vector).
- **Mock ligation control:** vector + ligase (tests dephosphorylation efficiency).
- **Untransformed cells** (sterility control).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Blue-White Screening Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **❌ Forgetting the role of dephosphorylation** — Most students omit CIP/SAP treatment, which is a major 1–2 mark loss because it is the *root cause* of high blue colony background.
> 2. **❌ Confusing X-gal (substrate) with IPTG (inducer)** — IPTG does NOT produce color; it only induces the gene. X-gal is the *chromogenic substrate*.
> 3. **❌ Stating that "X-gal is cleaved to form blue color"** without mentioning **β-galactosidase** and **atmospheric oxygen** as essential cofactors.
> 4. **❌ Skipping unit conversion** in calculation questions (e.g., 10 ng = 0.01 µg) — examiners strictly deduct 0.5 mark for incorrect units.
> 5. **❌ Saying "white = good"** without clarifying that **white colonies must be confirmed by colony PCR** to rule out false positives from frameshift mutations.
> 6. **❌ Omitting the need for the *lacZΔM15* host** — many answers forget that α-complementation *requires* a specific host genotype.
> 7. **❌ Wrong molar ratio** — saying 1:1 insert:vector is optimal, when the correct ratio is **3:1** to drive recombinant ligation kinetics.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **📋 KTU PCBBL407 — Blue-White Screening: High-Density Revision Notes**

✅ Blue-white screening is based on **insertional inactivation of *lacZα* gene** within the **MCS of pUC19/pUC18/pBluescript** vectors.

✅ Functional **β-galactosidase** = **α-peptide (plasmid) + ω-fragment (host chromosome)** → cleaves **X-gal** → **indigo blue** color.

✅ **White colonies = putative recombinants** (insert has disrupted *lacZα*); **Blue colonies = non-recombinants** (self-ligated vector).

✅ Standard working concentrations: **X-gal = 40 µg/mL**, **IPTG = 0.1 mM**, **Ampicillin = 100 µg/mL**.

✅ **IPTG is an inducer** (analog of allolactose), **NOT a substrate** — it de-represses the *lac* operon by binding LacI repressor.

✅ **X-gal is dissolved in DMF (not water)** because it is hydrophobic; stock = 20 mg/mL.

✅ Host strain: ***E. coli* DH5α** (or JM109, XL1-Blue) must carry ***lacZΔM15*** allele for α-complementation.

✅ **Dephosphorylation of vector (CIP or SAP)** is critical to prevent self-ligation and reduce blue colony background.

✅ Insert:vector molar ratio for ligation = **3:1**; ligation at **16 °C overnight** with T4 DNA ligase.

✅ Transformation by **heat shock (42 °C, 45 sec)** or **electroporation (1.8 kV)**.

✅ Incubation: **37 °C, 16–24 hours**, plates kept in **dark** (X-gal is light-sensitive).

✅ **Recombination Efficiency** = (White colonies / Total colonies) × 100% — a good ligation gives **> 80%**.

✅ **Transformation Efficiency** = (Total colonies × Resuspension volume) / (Plasmid mass × Plated volume) — acceptable range = **10⁶ – 10⁸ CFU/µg**.

✅ **Confirmation of white colonies** is MANDATORY: **colony PCR, plasmid miniprep + restriction digestion, or Sanger sequencing**.

✅ **Limitations:** Not 100% accurate; small inserts may not disrupt *lacZα*; in-frame insertions may still give color; requires specific host genotype.

✅ Plates should be wrapped in **aluminum foil** to prevent photobleaching of X-gal.

✅ The *lacZα* gene is **~50 amino acids**; the *lacZω* gene is provided by the **chromosomal *lacZΔM15*** allele.

✅ **Positive selection (Amp)** + **Negative/Visual selection (X-gal + IPTG)** = dual-screening system.

✅ Discovered via the **α-complementation** phenomenon from Jacob & Monod's *lac* operon studies (1965 Nobel Prize).

✅ **Alternative method:** Newer systems use **cspD-based blue-white screening** or **red-white screening** (with RFP) for hosts lacking *lacZΔM15*.

✅ **Never use BL21(DE3) as host** for blue-white screening — it lacks the *lacZΔM15* allele.

✅ **Ampicillin satellite colonies** are a common artifact — use fresh plates and proper Amp concentration to avoid this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Isolation of DNA Fragments by Restriction Digestion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBL407 - MOLECULAR BIOLOGY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Isolation of DNA Fragments by Restriction Digestion # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Restriction Digestion** is an in-vitro enzymatic process in which a purified **Type II Restriction Endonuclease (RE)** recognizes a specific short, double-stranded DNA recognition sequence (typically 4–8 base pairs) and catalyzes the hydrolytic cleavage of the phosphodiester backbone at a defined position within or adjacent to that site. The reaction yields a discrete set of DNA fragments of predictable sizes, which is the foundational step for isolating, mapping, and cloning specific DNA fragments. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Restriction digestion is the *gateway experiment* of the Molecular Biology Lab. Mastering the reaction setup (10× buffer, BSA, enzyme units, incubation temperature 37 °C) and result interpretation (band size, number of fragments, complete vs. partial digestion) carries the highest weightage in the lab record and viva voce. **Key Terminology Table (Board-Exam Ready):** | Term | Rigorous Definition | KTU Exam Cue | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Restriction Enzyme** | Bacterial endonuclease that cleaves dsDNA at specific sequences. | Always mention "Type II" unless specified. | | **Recognition Sequence** | Short dsDNA motif (4–8 bp) recognized by the RE. | Must be a **palindrome** in Type II enzymes. | | **Palindromic Sequence** | Sequence that reads the same on the complementary strand in 5′→3′. | Example: GAATTC, CTCGAG. | | **Sticky Ends (Cohesive Ends)** | 5′ or 3′ single-stranded overhangs generated by staggered cuts. | Named "sticky" because they can re-anneal. | | **Blunt Ends** | Both strands cut at the same phosphodiester bond, no overhang. | Formed by enzymes like *SmaI* (CCC↓GGG) and *EcoRV* (GAT↓ATC). | | **Star Activity** | Relaxed specificity of RE under non-optimal conditions (high glycerol, low salt, DMSO, excess enzyme). | Common cause of "unexpected extra bands" in lab reports. | | **Unit (U)** | Amount of enzyme that completely digests 1 µg of λ-DNA in 1 hour at the optimum temperature. | Standard definition used to calculate volume. | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Scissors" Model Imagine you have a **very long printed book** (the genomic DNA) and you need to extract a *specific chapter* (the gene of interest) without damaging its text. You cannot just tear the book randomly. Instead, you use a **laser-guided pair of scissors** (the restriction enzyme) that has been pre-programmed to recognize a *unique sequence of letters* (e.g., the word "GAATTC") and snip the paper *exactly* at that spot. - If the scissors cut **both sheets of paper at the same position**, you get a **clean, flat edge** → **Blunt ends**. - If the scissors cut the **two sheets at slightly offset positions**, you get **frayed, jagged edges** that can interlock with matching frayed edges from another piece → **Sticky ends**. To find the chapter, you don't read the book — you **measure the length of the resulting paper strips** (fragment size) and identify the one corresponding to your chapter. This measurement is done by **agarose gel electrophoresis**, where shorter strips migrate faster through a gel mesh, just as a small ball falls faster through a sand column than a large ball. > [!NOTE] > **Why does the bacterium even have these "scissors"?** Restriction enzymes are part of the **Restriction-Modification (R-M) System**, a bacterial innate immune defense that cleaves invading viral (phage) DNA while protecting its own DNA via methylation. The lab exploits this bacterial weapon as a precision molecular tool. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard incubation temperature** for most Type II REs: **37 °C** (mesophilic optimum). - **Heat-inactivation temperature** (to stop the reaction): **65 °C** for 15–20 min (for most common enzymes like *EcoRI*, *HindIII*, *BamHI*). - **Standard reaction time**: **1–2 hours** (overnight for difficult templates). - **Standard reaction volume**: **20–50 µL** in a 0.2 mL or 0.5 mL microcentrifuge tube. - **Enzyme concentration**: Typically **5–20 U/µg DNA** for genomic DNA, **2–5 U/µg** for plasmid DNA. - **Glycerol carry-over limit**: **<5% v/v** in the final reaction mix (to prevent star activity). # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — The 5-Pillar Mechanism of Restriction Digestion The digestion reaction is governed by five tightly coupled molecular events. Understanding the **"Why"** behind each step is what separates a topper from an average student in KTU viva. ### Pillar 1: Recognition Site Binding - The RE enzyme scans the DNA helix via **1D diffusion** (sliding) and **3D hopping** (jumping). - It binds specifically to its palindromic recognition sequence, inducing a **DNA kink** of ~20°. - **Why?** The enzyme's catalytic core contains conserved **PD…D/ExK** motifs that coordinate **two Mg²⁺ ions**, which activate the water molecule for nucleophilic attack on the phosphodiester bond. ### Pillar 2: Phosphodiester Bond Hydrolysis - The phosphodiester bond is cleaved via an **SN2 nucleophilic substitution** mechanism. - One Mg²⁺ activates the attacking water; the second Mg²⁺ stabilizes the pentacovalent transition state and the leaving 3′-O⁻ group. - **How?** A single RE cleaves *both* strands in a coordinated manner (unlike non-specific nucleases). ### Pillar 3: Generation of Fragment Ends - The geometry of the cut determines the end type: - **5′ overhang (sticky)**: e.g., *EcoRI* — 5′…G↓AATTC…3′ - **3′ overhang (sticky)**: e.g., *KpnI* — 5′…GGTAC↓C…3′ - **Blunt**: e.g., *SmaI* — 5′…CCC↓GGG…3′ - **Why does this matter for KTU?** Sticky-end ligations are far more efficient (annealing-driven) than blunt-end ligations, an answer that frequently appears in viva. ### Pillar 4: Buffer & Co-factor Chemistry - Every Type II RE has an **iso-tschermian optimum** for pH, ionic strength (NaCl/KCl), and Mg²⁺ concentration, codified in a **10× reaction buffer** (commonly 4 variants: low, medium, high salt + SAM for *Dam*-sensitive enzymes). - **BSA** is added to stabilize the enzyme and prevent surface adsorption to the tube wall. - **DTT** (dithiothreitol) maintains the active-site cysteine residues in a reduced state. ### Pillar 5: Termination of Reaction - Achieved by **heat inactivation** (65 °C, 20 min) or by adding **EDTA (10–20 mM)**, which chelates the Mg²⁺ cofactor, rendering the enzyme catalytically inactive. - **Why this matters**: If you skip this, the enzyme may continue to act in downstream ligation, destroying your vector. ## 2.2 Classification of Restriction Endonucleases (High-Yield) | Type | Structural Example | Cofactor | Cleavage Site | KTU Weightage | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Type I** | *EcoKI* | Mg²⁺, ATP, SAM | Random, >1000 bp away from recognition site | Low (definition only) | | **Type II** | *EcoRI*, *HindIII*, *BamHI* | Mg²⁺ only | Within/at recognition site | **VERY HIGH** | | **Type III** | *EcoP15I* | Mg²⁺, ATP | 20–30 bp downstream of recognition site | Low | | **Type IV** | *McrBC* | Mg²⁺, GTP, SAM | Modified DNA (methylated, hydroxymethylated) | Low | > [!TIP] > **Viva Shortcut**: For 95% of KTU questions, assume the question refers to **Type II** unless explicitly stated. Type II is the workhorse of molecular cloning. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Rule | Expression | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Number of fragments (linear DNA)** | $n_{frag} = N_{sites} + 1$ | For $N$ recognition sites on linear DNA | | **Number of fragments (circular DNA)** | $n_{frag} = N_{sites}$ | For $N$ recognition sites on plasmid/cosmid | | **Fragment size (linear, symmetric cleavage)** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i = L_{total}$ | Sum of all fragment lengths = original DNA length | | **Expected band intensity (equimolar)** | $I_i \propto \frac{1}{L_i}$ | Smaller fragments are brighter (more copies per µg) | | **Enzyme volume required** | $V_{enzyme} = \frac{U_{required}}{C_{stock}} \, \mu L$ | $C_{stock}$ typically 10–20 U/µL | | **Migration rate (agarose gel)** | $\log_{10}(L) = a - b \cdot d$ | $d$ = migration distance; $a, b$ are gel-specific constants | | **Recognition site frequency (random DNA)** | $f = \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{k}$ | For a $k$-bp recognition site, assuming equal nucleotide frequencies | | **Expected cut sites (random DNA)** | $E = L \cdot f$ | $L$ = total length in bp | | **Sticky end overhang length** | $n_{overhang} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ | Common: 4 nt (e.g., *EcoRI*) | | **Reaction time for complete digestion** | $t_{complete} \geq \frac{U_{enzyme}}{U_{required}} \cdot 1 \, hr$ | Per unit definition: 1 U = 1 µg λ-DNA in 1 hr | | **Buffer pH range (Type II RE)** | $7.0 - 8.5$ | Optimized at 25 °C | | **Standard Mg²⁺ concentration** | $5 - 10 \, mM$ | Essential catalytic cofactor | ## 2.4 Engineering & Real-World Utility The isolation of DNA fragments by restriction digestion is the **cornerstone technology** behind: 1. **Recombinant DNA Technology & Genetic Engineering** — Insertion of genes into plasmid vectors. 2. **DNA Fingerprinting & Forensic Science** — RFLP (Restriction Fragment Length Polymorphism) analysis for crime-scene identification. 3. **Medical Diagnostics** — Identification of disease-causing mutations (e.g., sickle-cell anemia, Huntington's disease) via altered RE cut patterns. 4. **Agricultural Biotechnology** — Development of Bt-crops, Golden Rice, and herbicide-resistant cultivars. 5. **Pharmacogenomics** — Personalized medicine based on patient-specific restriction patterns. 6. **Synthetic Biology & CRISPR Validation** — Confirming successful guide-RNA insertions by diagnostic digests. > [!NOTE] > **Industrial Scale**: The global market for restriction enzymes is valued at over **USD 500 million** annually, with **New England Biolabs (NEB), Thermo Fisher, and Promega** being the leading commercial suppliers. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of DNA Fragments from $N$ Restriction Sites **Problem Statement (KTU Style):** "A 48.5 kb linear bacteriophage λ-DNA has 5 recognition sites for *EcoRI*. How many fragments will be produced? If the same DNA is circular (a plasmid), what is the fragment count?" ### Step 1: Set Up the Topological Model A linear DNA molecule has **2 free ends** (a head and a tail). A circular DNA molecule has **0 free ends** (a closed loop). Every recognition site that is cut introduces **one new free end** on each side of the cut. The total number of fragments equals the total number of free ends divided by 2 (since each fragment is bounded by two ends). ### Step 2: Apply the General Theorem Let $N$ be the number of recognition sites that are cut, $E_{initial}$ be the number of pre-existing free ends, and $E_{cut}$ be the number of new free ends generated. $$E_{new} = E_{initial} + 2N$$ The number of fragments $F$ is given by: $$F = \frac{E_{new}}{2} = \frac{E_{initial} + 2N}{2} = \frac{E_{initial}}{2} + N$$ ### Step 3: Substitute for Linear DNA For linear DNA, $E_{initial} = 2$ (two natural termini). Substituting: $$F_{linear} = \frac{2}{2} + N = 1 + N$$ For $N = 5$: $$F_{linear} = 1 + 5 = 6 \, \text{fragments}$$ ### Step 4: Substitute for Circular DNA For circular DNA, $E_{initial} = 0$ (a closed loop has no free ends). Substituting: $$F_{circular} = \frac{0}{2} + N = N$$ For $N = 5$: $$F_{circular} = 5 \, \text{fragments}$$ ### Step 5: Verification via Sum Rule For the λ-DNA example, the actual *EcoRI* fragment sizes are known: **21.2, 7.4, 5.8, 5.6, 4.9, 3.5 kb** (6 fragments). Summing: $$\sum L_i = 21.2 + 7.4 + 5.8 + 5.6 + 4.9 + 3.5 = 48.4 \, \text{kb}$$ The minor discrepancy from 48.5 kb is due to rounding in published size data. The result confirms $F_{linear} = 6$. ✓ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always state *which topology* you are assuming. A 5-site cut yields 5 fragments on a plasmid but 6 fragments on a linear chromosome. A surprising number of students lose the 1 mark for forgetting the linear +1 term. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Expected Number of Recognition Sites in a Random DNA Sequence **Problem Statement:** "What is the expected number of *HindIII* (AAGCTT) recognition sites in a random 50 kb DNA sequence? What is the expected fragment size upon complete digestion?" ### Step 1: Define the Recognition Sequence Length *HindIII* recognizes a 6-bp palindrome: 5′-A↓A G C T T-3′. Thus, $k = 6$ bases. ### Step 2: Apply the Random Sequence Probability For a random DNA sequence with equal nucleotide frequencies ($P_A = P_T = P_G = P_C = 0.25$), the probability of finding a specific 6-bp motif at any given position is: $$f = \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{k} = \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{6}$$ $$f = \frac{1}{4096} \approx 2.44 \times 10^{-4}$$ ### Step 3: Calculate Expected Number of Sites The expected number of recognition sites $E$ in a DNA of length $L$ bp is: $$E = L \cdot f$$ For $L = 50,000$ bp: $$E = 50,000 \times 2.44 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$E \approx 12.2 \text{ sites}$$ ### Step 4: Calculate Expected Fragment Size For a linear DNA, the number of fragments is: $$F = E + 1 = 12.2 + 1 = 13.2 \, \text{fragments}$$ The expected *average* fragment size is: $$\bar{L} = \frac{L_{total}}{F} = \frac{50,000}{13.2} \approx 3,788 \, \text{bp} \approx 3.8 \, \text{kb}$$ ### Step 5: Generalization — The "4^k Rule" For any Type II RE with a $k$-bp recognition site, the expected spacing between sites is: $$\bar{D} = 4^{k} \, \text{bp}$$ | Enzyme | $k$ (bp) | $\bar{D}$ (Expected Mean Spacing) | Frequency | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | *TaqI* (TCGA) | 4 | 256 bp | Very frequent cutter | | *HindIII* (AAGCTT) | 6 | 4,096 bp | Moderate cutter | | *NotI* (GCGGCCGC) | 8 | 65,536 bp | Rare cutter | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is this important for KTU?** A 4-bp cutter (e.g., *AluI*, *TaqI*) generates a smear on a gel because the fragments are too small (100–500 bp) and too numerous (>100 bands) to resolve. A 6-bp cutter (e.g., *EcoRI*, *BamHI*) produces a manageable band pattern (5–20 bands). An 8-bp cutter (e.g., *NotI*, *SfiI*) produces very few, very large bands. This explains the *choice of enzyme* in mapping experiments. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Agarose Gel Migration & Size Estimation **Problem Statement:** "Two DNA samples, A and B, migrate 2.4 cm and 1.6 cm, respectively, in a 1% agarose gel at 100 V for 45 min. The size marker (λ-*HindIII* digest) shows the 4.3 kb band at 2.0 cm and the 2.0 kb band at 2.7 cm. Estimate the size of samples A and B." ### Step 1: Establish the Log-Linear Relationship The migration distance $d$ of a DNA fragment is inversely proportional to the logarithm of its size $L$ in base pairs: $$d = a - b \cdot \log_{10}(L)$$ where $a$ and $b$ are empirical constants for the given gel conditions. ### Step 2: Solve for the Constants Using the Two Markers From the marker data: $$2.0 = a - b \cdot \log_{10}(4300)$$ $$2.7 = a - b \cdot \log_{10}(2000)$$ Subtracting the first equation from the second: $$2.7 - 2.0 = -b \cdot \log_{10}(2000) + b \cdot \log_{10}(4300)$$ $$0.7 = b \cdot \left[\log_{10}(4300) - \log_{10}(2000)\right]$$ $$0.7 = b \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{4300}{2000}\right)$$ $$0.7 = b \cdot \log_{10}(2.15)$$ $$0.7 = b \cdot 0.3324$$ $$b = 2.106 \, \text{cm per log unit}$$ ### Step 3: Solve for $a$ Substituting back into the first marker equation: $$2.0 = a - 2.106 \cdot \log_{10}(4300)$$ $$2.0 = a - 2.106 \cdot 3.6335$$ $$2.0 = a - 7.652$$ $$a = 9.652 \, \text{cm}$$ ### Step 4: Estimate Size of Sample A ($d_A = 2.4$ cm) $$2.4 = 9.652 - 2.106 \cdot \log_{10}(L_A)$$ $$2.106 \cdot \log_{10}(L_A) = 9.652 - 2.4 = 7.252$$ $$\log_{10}(L_A) = \frac{7.252}{2.106} = 3.443$$ $$L_A = 10^{3.443} \approx 2774 \, \text{bp} \approx 2.8 \, \text{kb}$$ ### Step 5: Estimate Size of Sample B ($d_B = 1.6$ cm) $$1.6 = 9.652 - 2.106 \cdot \log_{10}(L_B)$$ $$2.106 \cdot \log_{10}(L_B) = 9.652 - 1.6 = 8.052$$ $$\log_{10}(L_B) = \frac{8.052}{2.106} = 3.823$$ $$L_B = 10^{3.823} \approx 6655 \, \text{bp} \approx 6.7 \, \text{kb}$$ ### Step 6: Verification Sample A is smaller → migrated further → **2.8 kb** ✓ Sample B is larger → migrated less → **6.7 kb** ✓ The results are physically consistent. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** Many students write the formula as $L = a - b \cdot d$ (linear) instead of $\log_{10}(L) = a - b \cdot d$ (log-linear). This causes an answer off by **2–3 orders of magnitude**. Always remember: the relationship is **semi-log**, not linear. ## 3.4 Standard Reaction Composition (Analytical Matrix) The table below gives the **master-mix** for a 20 µL standard restriction digestion of 1 µg of plasmid DNA, as required in the KTU 2024 lab syllabus. | Component | Stock Concentration | Volume per 20 µL | Final Concentration | Function | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Nuclease-free water | — | 16.3 µL | — | Bulk volume | | 10× Reaction buffer | 10× | 2.0 µL | 1× | Maintains pH, ionic strength | | BSA (optional) | 100× (10 mg/mL) | 0.2 µL | 1× (0.1 mg/mL) | Enzyme stabilizer | | DNA substrate | Variable | 1.0 µL | 50 ng/µL | Substrate | | Restriction enzyme | 10 U/µL | 0.5 µL | 0.25 U/µL | Catalyst | | **Total** | — | **20.0 µL** | — | — | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Complete Restriction Digestion Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Purified DNA sample] --> B[Quantify DNA
using Nanodrop / Spectrophotometer] B --> C[Calculate required volume
for 1 microgram DNA] C --> D[Prepare 20 microlitre reaction in 0.2 mL tube] D --> D1[Add 16.3 microlitre nuclease-free water] D1 --> D2[Add 2.0 microlitre 10X buffer] D2 --> D3[Add 0.2 microlitre 100X BSA] D3 --> D4[Add 1.0 microlitre DNA
equals 1 microgram] D4 --> D5[Add 0.5 microlitre restriction enzyme
equals 5 units] D5 --> E[Gently mix by flicking
Do NOT vortex] E --> F[Brief spin down in microfuge] F --> G[Incubate at 37 degrees Celsius
for 1 to 2 hours] G --> H{Complete digestion?} H -- No --> I[Extend incubation to 4 hours
or overnight at 37 C] I --> H H -- Yes --> J[Heat inactivate at 65 C
for 20 minutes] J --> K[Add 4 microlitre 6X gel loading dye] K --> L[Load onto 1 percent agarose gel
with EtBr or SYBR Safe] L --> M[Run gel at 100 V for 45 to 60 minutes] M --> N[Visualize under UV / Blue light transilluminator] N --> O[Photograph and analyze band pattern] O --> P[Compare with DNA size marker
to estimate fragment sizes] P --> Q[End: Isolated DNA fragment ready for downstream use] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of a Type II RE Reaction ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input[Input Module] I1[Double stranded DNA
48.5 kb lambda phage] I2[Recognition sequence
GAATTC palindrome] I3[10X Reaction buffer
pH 7.9, 50 mM NaCl] end subgraph Catalyst[Enzymatic Module] E1[EcoRI homodimer
2 X 31 kDa subunits] E2[Mg2+ cofactor
2 ions per active site] E3[Active site motif
PD and D/ExK] end subgraph Chemistry[Catalytic Chemistry] C1[DNA binding
induces 20 degree kink] C2[Nucleophilic attack
on phosphodiester bond] C3[SN2 transition state
pentacovalent phosphorus] C4[Cleavage yields
5 prime overhang AATT] end subgraph Output[Output Module] O1[Fragment 1
21.2 kb with sticky end] O2[Fragment 2
7.4 kb with sticky end] O3[Fragment N
3.5 kb with sticky end] end I1 --> E1 I2 --> C1 I3 --> E1 E1 --> C1 E2 --> C2 E3 --> C2 C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 C4 --> O1 C4 --> O2 C4 --> O3 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Tree for Diagnosing Failed Digestion ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Digestion gel shows unexpected pattern] --> Q1{Number of bands
correct?} Q1 -- Too few bands --> A1[Incomplete digestion] Q1 -- Too many bands --> A2[Star activity or contamination] Q1 -- Smear --> A3[DNA degradation by nucleases] Q1 -- No bands --> A4[No DNA loaded or wrong buffer] A1 --> A11[Check enzyme expiry date] A1 --> A12[Increase incubation to 4 hours] A1 --> A13[Add 2X enzyme concentration] A1 --> A14[Verify buffer pH and Mg2+] A2 --> A21[Check glycerol carry over less than 5 percent] A2 --> A22[Avoid DMSO and ethanol] A2 --> A23[Use fresh high quality buffer] A2 --> A24[Reduce enzyme to 1 to 2 U per microgram] A3 --> A31[Use nuclease free water] A3 --> A32[Add RNase and DNase inhibitors] A3 --> A33[Check sterile technique] A3 --> A34[Run undigested control on same gel] A4 --> A41[Re-quantify DNA on Nanodrop] A4 --> A42[Verify pipetting accuracy] A4 --> A43[Check gel loading and electrophoresis] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why the Decision Tree?** In the KTU 2024 lab viva, students are often shown a real (or simulated) failed gel and asked to diagnose the cause. Memorizing this diagnostic flow earns 3–5 viva marks with minimal effort. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** **"Define restriction endonuclease. With a suitable example, explain the terms 'palindromic sequence' and 'sticky ends'."** **Model Answer (Concise Board-Ready Version):** A **restriction endonuclease** is a bacterial enzyme that recognizes and cleaves double-stranded DNA at specific short nucleotide sequences called recognition sites. *Palindromic sequence* — A sequence that reads the same on both strands in the 5′→3′ direction. *Example:* The *EcoRI* recognition sequence is $$\text{5'-G A A T T C-3'}$$ $$\text{3'-C T T A A G-5'}$$ Reading the bottom strand 5′→3′ gives G-A-A-T-T-C, identical to the top strand. *Sticky ends* — *EcoRI* cleaves between G and A, leaving 5′-AATT overhangs: $$\text{5'-G A A T T C-3'}$$ $$\text{3'-C T T A A G-5'}$$ These single-stranded overhangs are "sticky" because they can hydrogen-bond with complementary sticky ends from any other DNA cut with the same enzyme, enabling fragment joining in cloning. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]** **"Differentiate between Type II and Type III restriction enzymes. Why is Type II preferred in molecular cloning?"** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Type II | Type III | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Recognition site | 4–8 bp, palindromic | 5–7 bp, non-palindromic | | Cleavage location | Within recognition site | 20–30 bp downstream | | Cofactor requirement | Mg²⁺ only | Mg²⁺ + ATP | | Structural complexity | Simple homodimer | Multi-subunit complex | | Predictability of cut | Highly predictable | Less predictable | Type II is preferred in molecular cloning because **(i)** the cut occurs *within* the recognition site, giving **predictable fragment sizes**; **(ii)** only **Mg²⁺** is required as a cofactor, simplifying the reaction; **(iii)** they produce **defined ends** (sticky or blunt) that are easy to ligate; and **(iv)** they are **commercially available** in highly pure, well-characterized forms. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Format ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Slot 1]** **(a)** Describe the principle and procedure for the isolation of DNA fragments by restriction digestion. Add a note on the components of a standard reaction mixture. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Principle (2 marks):** Restriction endonucleases recognize specific palindromic sequences in double-stranded DNA and cleave the phosphodiester backbone at a defined position. When a DNA molecule containing multiple copies of the recognition site is incubated with the appropriate enzyme, it is cut into a discrete number of fragments whose sizes can be predicted from the relative positions of the cut sites. These fragments can then be separated and visualized by agarose gel electrophoresis. **Procedure (3 marks):** 1. Quantify the DNA sample using a spectrophotometer (A₂₆₀) and check purity (A₂₆₀/A₂₈₀ ≈ 1.8–2.0). 2. In a sterile 0.2 mL microcentrifuge tube, assemble the reaction on ice: 1 µg DNA, 2 µL of 10× reaction buffer, 0.2 µL BSA, nuclease-free water to 19.5 µL. 3. Add 0.5 µL (5 U) of restriction enzyme. Always add the enzyme last. 4. Mix gently by flicking the tube (do **not** vortex, as this can denature the enzyme). 5. Pulse-spin the tube in a microfuge to collect the contents. 6. Incubate at **37 °C** for 1–2 hours in a water bath or dry block heater. 7. Heat inactivate the enzyme at **65 °C** for 20 minutes (this step is critical to prevent star activity or downstream cleavage in ligation reactions). 8. Add 4 µL of 6× gel loading dye. 9. Load the entire sample on a 0.8–1.2% agarose gel containing ethidium bromide (0.5 µg/mL) alongside a DNA size marker (e.g., λ-*HindIII* or 1 kb ladder). 10. Run at 80–100 V for 45–60 minutes in 1× TAE or 1× TBE buffer. 11. Visualize under UV transillumination and photograph. **Reaction Mixture Components (2 marks):** | Component | Function | | :--- | :--- | | DNA substrate | The molecule to be cleaved | | 10× buffer | Provides optimal pH, ionic strength, Mg²⁺ | | BSA | Stabilizes enzyme, prevents tube adsorption | | Restriction enzyme | Catalyst | | Nuclease-free water | Bulk solvent | **(b)** A 12.0 kb linear DNA molecule has 3 sites for *BamHI* and 2 sites for *HindIII*. Predict the number of fragments obtained when the DNA is digested with (i) *BamHI* alone, (ii) *HindIII* alone, and (iii) both *BamHI* and *HindIII* together. Assume the sites are non-overlapping. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) Digestion with *BamHI* alone (2 marks):** For a linear DNA, number of fragments = $N + 1$, where $N$ is the number of recognition sites. $$F_{BamHI} = 3 + 1 = 4 \, \text{fragments}$$ **(ii) Digestion with *HindIII* alone (2 marks):** $$F_{HindIII} = 2 + 1 = 3 \, \text{fragments}$$ **(iii) Double digestion with *BamHI* + *HindIII* (3 marks):** Total number of recognition sites (assuming non-overlapping): $$N_{total} = 3 + 2 = 5$$ Number of fragments for linear DNA: $$F_{double} = 5 + 1 = 6 \, \text{fragments}$$ **Verification of total length (Optional, 1 mark):** If the individual fragment sizes are A, B, C from *HindIII* and P, Q, R, S from *BamHI*, then in the double digest we will see 6 bands whose sizes are sub-segments of A, B, C cut by the *BamHI* sites lying within them. The sum of all 6 double-digest fragments still equals **12.0 kb**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Main Slot 2]** **(a)** Explain in detail the nomenclature and classification of restriction enzymes. List the key features of Type II enzymes that make them indispensable in genetic engineering. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Nomenclature (2.5 marks):** Restriction enzymes are named according to a standardized rule established by Smith and Nathans (1973): - The **first letter** (capital) is the genus of the source organism. *Example:* **E** for *Escherichia*. - The **next two letters** (lowercase) are the species. *Example:* **co** for *coli*. - If the organism has multiple strains or phages, a **strain/serotype letter or number** follows. *Example:* **R** for RY13 strain. - A **Roman numeral** denotes the order of discovery. *Example:* **I** for the first enzyme from that strain. *Worked example:* *EcoRI* = **E**scherichia **co**li, strain **R**Y13, **I**st enzyme discovered. Another example: *HindIII* = **H**aemophilus **in**fluenzae, strain **d**, **III**rd enzyme. **Classification (2 marks):** Restriction enzymes are classified into **four types (I, II, III, IV)** based on: - Subunit composition - Cofactor requirements - Position of cleavage relative to the recognition site - Sequence specificity Type I (e.g., *EcoKI*) — multi-subunit, ATP + SAM dependent, cuts far from recognition site. Type II (e.g., *EcoRI*) — homodimer, Mg²⁺ only, cuts within recognition site, palindromic. Type III (e.g., *EcoP15I*) — multi-subunit, ATP + Mg²⁺, cuts 20–30 bp downstream. Type IV — cuts modified DNA (e.g., methylated). **Key Features of Type II Enzymes in Genetic Engineering (2.5 marks):** 1. **Absolute site specificity** — Every molecule cut at the same site, giving reproducible fragments. 2. **Defined end structures** — Sticky or blunt ends, predictable and ligatable. 3. **Simple cofactor requirement** — Only Mg²⁺ is needed; no ATP/SAM. 4. **Commercial availability** — Highly purified, well-characterized, affordable. 5. **Wide variety of specificities** — Over 600 Type II REs are commercially available, recognizing >250 distinct sequences. 6. **Isoschizomers and neoschizomers** — Same recognition site, different cut site (or vice versa), offering flexibility in cloning design. **(b)** A circular plasmid of 5.2 kb is digested with three different enzymes in separate reactions. The observed fragment sizes are: - *EcoRI* alone: 3.4 kb and 1.8 kb - *HindIII* alone: 5.2 kb (single band) - *BamHI* alone: 2.7 kb and 2.5 kb Construct a restriction map showing the relative positions of all three sites. Show all calculations. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Determine the number of sites for each enzyme (1 mark):** For a circular DNA, the number of fragments equals the number of sites. - *EcoRI*: 2 fragments → **2 sites** (3.4 kb and 1.8 kb) - *HindIII*: 1 fragment (5.2 kb) → **1 site** - *BamHI*: 2 fragments → **2 sites** (2.7 kb and 2.5 kb) **Step 2: Construct the circular map (3 marks):** Since *HindIII* has only 1 site, it provides the natural **0-position reference** on the circular map. Place the *HindIII* site at position 0. Place the *EcoRI* sites. The two fragments from *EcoRI* alone are 3.4 kb and 1.8 kb, summing to 5.2 kb. Thus, the two *EcoRI* sites are **1.8 kb apart** (and 3.4 kb apart on the other arc). Place the *BamHI* sites. The two fragments are 2.7 kb and 2.5 kb, summing to 5.2 kb. The two *BamHI* sites are **2.5 kb apart** (and 2.7 kb apart on the other arc). **Step 3: Determine relative positions via double digestion (3 marks):** To fix the map, perform a double digest. The standard result is: - *EcoRI* + *HindIII*: 3.0 kb, 1.4 kb, 0.8 kb - *BamHI* + *HindIII*: 3.1 kb, 2.1 kb - *EcoRI* + *BamHI*: 1.9 kb, 1.6 kb, 0.9 kb, 0.8 kb Using the *EcoRI* + *HindIII* data (1 mark): - One *EcoRI* site is **3.0 kb from *HindIII*** - The other *EcoRI* site is **0.8 + 1.4 = 2.2 kb from *HindIII*** on the other side - Sanity check: 3.0 + 2.2 = 5.2 kb ✓ Using the *BamHI* + *HindIII* data (1 mark): - One *BamHI* site is **3.1 kb from *HindIII*** - The other *BamHI* site is **2.1 kb from *HindIII*** on the other side - Sanity check: 3.1 + 2.1 = 5.2 kb ✓ Using the *EcoRI* + *BamHI* data (1 mark): - 4 fragments (2 *EcoRI* + 2 *BamHI* = 4 sites on circular → 4 fragments ✓) - The order of sites around the circle is finalized: *HindIII* (0) → *EcoRI*₁ (0.8 kb) → *EcoRI*₂ (1.6 kb) → *BamHI*₁ (1.9 kb) → *BamHI*₂ (3.1 kb) → back to *HindIII* (5.2 kb) **Final Map (Textual Representation, included in answer):** $$\text{HindIII} \xrightarrow{0.8} \text{EcoRI}_1 \xrightarrow{0.8} \text{EcoRI}_2 \xrightarrow{0.3} \text{BamHI}_1 \xrightarrow{1.2} \text{BamHI}_2 \xrightarrow{2.1} \text{HindIII}$$ Distances (kb): 0.8 + 0.8 + 0.3 + 1.2 + 2.1 = 5.2 kb ✓ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Don't confuse linear and circular formulas** — Circular = $N$ sites → $N$ fragments, NOT $N+1$. Many students write 3 for *EcoRI* and lose 1 mark. > 2. **Don't forget to sum the fragments** to verify they equal the total plasmid size. A 5.2 kb plasmid must have fragments summing to 5.2 kb. If they don't, an arithmetic error has occurred. > 3. **Don't omit the *HindIII* single-band result** in the analysis — it tells you there is exactly ONE *HindIII* site, which fixes the map's reference frame. > 4. **Always state the topology** ("the DNA is circular, so…") before applying the formula. Examiners give 1 mark for this. > 5. **In nomenclature questions**, students often forget the Roman numeral rule and write "*EcoR1*" instead of "*EcoRI*". This is a full 0.5 mark deduction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Molecular Biology Lab (Experiment 2)** ### A. Must-Know Definitions - ☐ **Restriction Endonuclease**: Bacterial enzyme that cleaves dsDNA at specific palindromic sites. - ☐ **Recognition Sequence**: 4–8 bp palindromic motif (e.g., GAATTC for *EcoRI*). - ☐ **Sticky End**: 5′ or 3′ single-stranded overhang. - ☐ **Blunt End**: Both strands cut at the same position, no overhang. - ☐ **Type II RE**: Mg²⁺-dependent, cuts within recognition site, palindromic. - ☐ **1 Unit (U)**: Amount of enzyme that completely digests 1 µg of λ-DNA in 1 hour at 37 °C. - ☐ **Star Activity**: Non-specific cleavage under suboptimal conditions (high glycerol, low salt, DMSO, excess enzyme). - ☐ **Heat Inactivation**: 65 °C for 20 min to stop the reaction. ### B. Must-Know Formulas - ☐ **Linear DNA fragments** = $N + 1$ sites. - ☐ **Circular DNA fragments** = $N$ sites. - ☐ **Random site frequency** = $\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{k}$ for a $k$-bp site. - ☐ **Expected spacing between sites** = $4^{k}$ bp. - ☐ **Gel migration** = $\log_{10}(L) = a - b \cdot d$ (semi-log, NOT linear). - ☐ **Sum of fragments = Total DNA length** (always verify this). - ☐ **Enzyme volume** = Units required / Stock concentration. ### C. Must-Know Examples - ☐ *EcoRI*: 5′-G↓AATTC-3′ → 4-nt 5′ sticky ends. - ☐ *HindIII*: 5′-A↓AGCTT-3′ → 4-nt 5′ sticky ends. - ☐ *BamHI*: 5′-G↓GATCC-3′ → 4-nt 5′ sticky ends. - ☐ *SmaI*: 5′-CCC↓GGG-3′ → Blunt ends. - ☐ *NotI*: 5′-GC↓GGCCGC-3′ → 4-nt 5′ sticky ends, 8-bp recognition (rare cutter). - ☐ *TaqI*: 5′-T↓CGA-3′ → 2-nt 5′ sticky ends, 4-bp recognition (frequent cutter). ### D. Must-Know Procedure - ☐ Add nuclease-free water first, then 10× buffer, then BSA, then DNA, **enzyme last**. - ☐ Mix by **flicking** (not vortexing). - ☐ Incubate at **37 °C for 1–2 hours**. - ☐ Heat inactivate at **65 °C for 20 minutes**. - ☐ Add 6× loading dye and load on **0.8–1.2% agarose gel** with EtBr/SYBR Safe. - ☐ Run at **80–100 V for 45–60 min** in 1× TAE/TBE. - ☐ Visualize under **UV transillumination** (EtBr) or **blue light** (SYBR Safe). ### E. Must-Know Viva Questions - ☐ *Why is BSA added?* → Enzyme stabilization, prevents surface adsorption. - ☐ *Why is the enzyme added last?* → To prevent premature activity at room temperature and to maintain its glycerol-rich storage buffer's integrity until the final mix is ready. - ☐ *Why is the reaction incubated at 37 °C?* → Optimum temperature for most mesophilic Type II REs (matches the human body and *E. coli* growth temperature). - ☐ *What is star activity and how is it prevented?* → Non-specific cleavage due to suboptimal conditions; prevented by using correct buffer, low glycerol (<5%), and minimal enzyme units. - ☐ *Why are Type II enzymes preferred over Type I?* → Simple cofactor requirement, predictable cut site, defined ends. > **Final Examiner's Tip:** A perfect KTU 2024 lab answer on this topic always includes: **(1)** the enzyme name with its 5′→3′ recognition sequence, **(2)** the type of end produced, **(3)** the correct fragment-count formula with topology stated, and **(4)** a verification step (sum of fragments = total DNA length). Master this four-part structure, and full marks are guaranteed.

topics.title: DNA Ligation of Isolated Genes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL407 - MOLECULAR BIOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** DNA Ligation of Isolated Genes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**DNA Ligation** is the enzymatic process of forming phosphodiester bonds between adjacent 3′-hydroxyl (3′-OH) and 5′-phosphate (5′-PO₄) groups of double-stranded DNA molecules, catalysed by the enzyme **DNA ligase**. In recombinant DNA technology, ligation is the molecular "gluing" step that covalently joins a foreign DNA fragment (insert) into a cloning vector (plasmid) to generate a recombinant DNA molecule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCBBL407 - Module 2):**
> "DNA ligation is the process of joining two DNA molecules (insert and vector) using DNA ligase to form a single recombinant DNA molecule. The reaction forms a phosphodiester bond between the 3′-OH of one nucleotide and the 5′-PO₄ of the adjacent nucleotide, requiring ATP (T4 DNA ligase) or NAD⁺ (E. coli DNA ligase) as a cofactor."

### 1.2 Types of DNA Ends to be Ligated

| End Type | Description | Overhang | Ligase Efficiency |
|----------|-------------|----------|-------------------|
| **Cohesive (Sticky) Ends** | 4-nt 5′ or 3′ single-stranded overhangs | 5′ or 3′ | High (T4 ligase) |
| **Blunt Ends** | Fully base-paired, no overhang | None | Lower (requires higher enzyme + PEG) |
| **TA Overhangs** | Single 3′-A overhang (PCR product) | 3′ T/A | High (T4 ligase) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Molecular Velcro + Glue" Model:**
> Imagine you have two pieces of a jigsaw puzzle (the insert and the vector) that have been cut with matching zigzag edges (sticky ends). The matching edges naturally *find each other* and *hold loosely* together — this is **annealing** (like Velcro snapping). However, the puzzle is not yet permanently connected. The **DNA ligase enzyme** is the **"welder"** that permanently fuses the two pieces by forming a covalent **phosphodiester bond**, just like a welder sealing two metal plates. Without ligase, your recombinant DNA would fall apart under any physical or thermal stress. With ligase, you get a stable, covalently closed circular recombinant plasmid ready for transformation.

### 1.4 Key Players in a Ligation Reaction

- **Insert DNA** — the gene of interest (often from a PCR product or restriction digest).
- **Vector DNA** — the plasmid backbone (e.g., pUC19, pET-28a) linearised with compatible ends.
- **T4 DNA Ligase** — the workhorse enzyme (from bacteriophage T4).
- **ATP** — the energy cofactor (consumed: 1 ATP per phosphodiester bond).
- **Buffer system** — Tris-HCl (pH 7.5–8.0), MgCl₂, DTT, with or without PEG.
- **DTT** — reduces disulphide bonds and protects ligase from oxidation.
- **BSA** (Bovine Serum Albumin) — stabilises the enzyme and prevents surface adsorption.

### 1.5 Physical / Standard Metrics

- **Optimal Temperature:** **16 °C overnight** (sticky ends) or **20–25 °C, 30 min** (rapid ligation).
- **Blunt-end Ligation:** **14–16 °C overnight** (slower kinetics).
- **Standard Ligation Volume:** **10 µL – 20 µL** (typical lab).
- **Enzyme Amount:** **1 Weiss Unit per 20 µL** (NEB T4 ligase).
- **Vector:Insert Molar Ratio:** **1:3** (most common) or **1:1** (blunt).
- **Co-factor:** **1 mM ATP** (final).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chemical Reaction Catalysed by DNA Ligase

DNA ligase catalyses a three-step nucleotidyl transfer reaction:

1. **Adenylation of Ligase** — Ligase attacks the α-phosphate of ATP (or the AMP moiety of NAD⁺), releasing pyrophosphate (PPi) or nicotinamide mononucleotide (NMN), and forming a **ligase–AMP intermediate** (covalently attached via a Lys-NH–P bond).
2. **Adenylation of DNA** — The AMP is transferred to the 5′-PO₄ end of the DNA, forming **DNA-5′-AMP** (5′ → 5′ phosphoanhydride).
3. **Seal** — The 3′-OH of the adjacent nucleotide attacks the 5′-PO₄, displacing AMP and forming the **phosphodiester bond**.

### 2.2 Mechanism — Why T4 vs E. coli Ligases Differ

| Feature | T4 DNA Ligase | E. coli DNA Ligase |
|---------|---------------|---------------------|
| Source | Bacteriophage T4 | *E. coli* |
| Co-factor | **ATP** | **NAD⁺** |
| Substrate range | Sticky, blunt, nicked DNA | Nicked dsDNA only (poor on sticky/blunt) |
| Km (ATP) | ~5 µM | — (uses NAD⁺) |
| Practical use | Universal cloning | Native nick repair |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Pearl:** E. coli DNA ligase **CANNOT** ligate blunt or sticky ends efficiently — it is restricted to nicks in dsDNA. Hence, T4 DNA ligase is the universal choice in rDNA technology.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a Ligation Reaction

**Why each step is necessary (the "Why and How"):**

1. **Vector linearisation** — Restriction digest opens the plasmid. Required because circular dsDNA cannot accept new genetic material without a break.
2. **Dephosphorylation (optional)** — Alkaline Phosphatase (CIP or SAP) removes 5′-PO₄ from the vector to **prevent self-ligation** (vector re-circularising without insert).
3. **Calculation of insert and vector concentrations** — Ensures the right stoichiometry for efficient intermolecular ligation.
4. **Mixing of insert + vector + buffer + ATP + ligase** — Annealing of complementary overhangs occurs first (if sticky), followed by enzymatic sealing.
5. **Incubation** — Allows the ligase catalytic cycle to proceed to completion.
6. **Heat inactivation (65 °C, 10 min)** — Stops the reaction and inactivates the enzyme (important before transformation to avoid killing cells).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Equations for Ligation Stoichiometry**

**Formula 1 — DNA Molecular Weight (dsDNA):**

$$M_{\text{dsDNA}} = N \times 660 \text{ Da/bp}$$

where $N$ is the number of base pairs and **660 Da/bp** is the average molecular weight of a base pair (for dsDNA).

**Formula 2 — Moles of DNA Ends (pmol):**

$$n_{\text{pmol}} = \frac{\text{mass (ng)}}{M_{\text{dsDNA}} \text{ (Da)}} \times 10^3$$

(Note: $1 \text{ ng} / N \text{ bp} \times 1000 = \text{pmol of DNA ends}$ when the shortcut form is used.)

**Formula 3 — Working Shortcut (for dsDNA):**

$$n_{\text{pmol}} = \frac{\text{ng of DNA}}{N_{\text{bp}} \times 0.66}$$

**Formula 4 — Required Mass of Insert for a Given Ratio:**

$$m_{\text{insert}} = m_{\text{vector}} \times \frac{N_{\text{insert}}}{N_{\text{vector}}} \times R$$

where:
- $m_{\text{vector}}$ = mass of vector (ng)
- $N_{\text{insert}}$, $N_{\text{vector}}$ = sizes in bp
- $R$ = desired **insert:vector molar ratio** (typically 3 for sticky ends, 5–10 for blunt).

**Formula 5 — Mass Conversion for Plasmid Prep Yields (typical):**

$$m_{\text{vector (ng)}} = \frac{C_{\text{vector}} (\text{ng/µL}) \times V_{\text{vector}} (\text{µL})}{1}$$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Biotechnological Utility

- **Recombinant protein production** — Ligation of an ORF into an expression vector (e.g., pET-28a → *E. coli* expression).
- **Gene therapy vectors** — AAV, lentiviral, and adenoviral genome assembly.
- **Synthetic biology / BioBricks** — Standardised assembly (Gibson, Golden Gate) builds on ligase chemistry.
- **DNA vaccines** — Plasmid-based vaccines (e.g., ZyCoV-D) require precise ligation of antigen-encoding genes.
- **CRISPR construct assembly** — sgRNA cassettes and Cas9 expression units are ligated into delivery vectors.
- **Industrial enzymes (chymosin, lipases)** — Produced commercially using ligated recombinant plasmids in *Aspergillus* or *Pichia*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Ligation Stoichiometry Calculation (Board-style)

**Problem (KTU Style):**
A researcher linearises **100 ng of pUC19** (size **2,686 bp**) with EcoRI. The insert is a **1,200 bp gene fragment** with compatible EcoRI ends. Calculate the **mass of insert (in ng)** required for a ligation at an **insert:vector molar ratio of 3:1**.

**Solution — Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation:**

Step 1 — Identify the variables.

$$m_{\text{vector}} = 100 \text{ ng}, \quad N_{\text{vector}} = 2686 \text{ bp}, \quad N_{\text{insert}} = 1200 \text{ bp}, \quad R = 3$$

Step 2 — Apply the working formula (Formula 4):

$$m_{\text{insert}} = m_{\text{vector}} \times \frac{N_{\text{insert}}}{N_{\text{vector}}} \times R$$

Step 3 — Substitute the values:

$$m_{\text{insert}} = 100 \times \frac{1200}{2686} \times 3$$

Step 4 — Compute the fraction:

$$\frac{1200}{2686} = 0.4468$$

Step 5 — Multiply by 100 and 3:

$$m_{\text{insert}} = 100 \times 0.4468 \times 3 = 134.04 \text{ ng}$$

Step 6 — Round to practical lab accuracy:

$$\boxed{m_{\text{insert}} \approx 134 \text{ ng}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** To ligate 100 ng of a 2.7 kb vector with a 1.2 kb insert at 3:1 molar ratio, you need ~134 ng of insert. Always add the insert in 2× or 3× this amount empirically and test.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Converting ng of DNA to pmol of Ends

**Problem:**
You have **500 ng of a 5,000 bp linear dsDNA fragment**. Calculate the **pmol of DNA ends**.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Recall the dsDNA MW:

$$M_{\text{dsDNA}} = N \times 660 = 5000 \times 660 = 3,300,000 \text{ Da} = 3.3 \times 10^6 \text{ g/mol}$$

Step 2 — Compute moles:

$$n_{\text{mol}} = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{500 \times 10^{-9} \text{ g}}{3.3 \times 10^6 \text{ g/mol}} = 1.515 \times 10^{-13} \text{ mol}$$

Step 3 — Convert to picomoles (1 pmol = 10⁻¹² mol):

$$n_{\text{pmol}} = 1.515 \times 10^{-13} \times 10^{12} = 0.1515 \text{ pmol}$$

Step 4 — Apply the shortcut formula (Formula 3) for verification:

$$n_{\text{pmol}} = \frac{\text{ng}}{N_{\text{bp}} \times 0.66} = \frac{500}{5000 \times 0.66} = \frac{500}{3300} = 0.1515 \text{ pmol}$$

$$\boxed{n_{\text{pmol}} \approx 0.152 \text{ pmol}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** 0.152 pmol of ends = 0.152 pmol of DNA molecules (each linear molecule has 2 ends but we count per molecule for ligation, not per end).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Ligation Reaction Recipe (Standard 20 µL)

| Component | Stock Concentration | Final Concentration | Volume (µL) |
|-----------|--------------------|---------------------|-------------|
| 10× T4 Ligase Buffer | 10× | 1× | 2.0 |
| Vector DNA (50 ng/µL) | — | ~25–50 ng | 1.0 |
| Insert DNA | — | 3:1 molar | varies |
| T4 DNA Ligase (400 U/µL) | 400 U/µL | 1–3 U | 1.0 |
| ATP (10 mM, optional) | 10 mM | 1 mM | 2.0 |
| Nuclease-free H₂O | — | to 20 µL | qs |
| **Total** | — | — | **20.0 µL** |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Tree — Choosing Ligation Conditions

| Scenario | Recommended Condition | Why |
|----------|----------------------|-----|
| Sticky ends (compatible) | 16 °C, overnight OR 25 °C, 30 min | Best efficiency |
| Blunt ends | 16 °C, overnight + 5–10% PEG | Slower kinetics, PEG promotes macro-molecular crowding |
| TA cloning (PCR product) | 22 °C, 1 h | T-A overhangs are partially annealed |
| Single insert + dephosphorylated vector | 1:3 vector:insert | High insert uptake, low background |
| Multiple inserts / library | 1:1 to 1:2 ratio | Prevents bias against large inserts |
| Self-ligation is a problem | Add CIP/SAP to vector | Removes 5′-PO₄, blocks self-ligation |
| Low efficiency observed | Increase insert amount, add fresh ATP | Common troubleshooting fix |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Ligation Reaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Insert DNA Fragment with 5 Prime PO4 and 3 Prime OH] --> C[Annealing of Complementary Cohesive Ends]
    B[Linearised Vector with 5 Prime PO4 and 3 Prime OH] --> C
    C --> D[DNA Ligase AMP Intermediate Formation]
    D --> E[Adenylation of 5 Prime Phosphate of DNA]
    E --> F[Attack by 3 Prime OH Nucleophile]
    F --> G[Phosphodiester Bond Formation]
    G --> H[Release of AMP]
    H --> I[Stable Covalently Closed Recombinant DNA]
    I --> J[Heat Inactivation at 65 C for 10 min]
    J --> K[Ready for Transformation into Competent Cells]
    L[ATP Co-factor] --> D
    M[Mg2 plus ions from Buffer] --> E
    N[Tris HCl Buffer pH 7.5 to 8.0] --> E
    O[DTT Reducing Agent] --> D
```

### 4.2 Ligation Decision Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start with Insert and Vector] --> S2{End Compatibility Check}
    S2 -->|Same Site or Compatible Overhangs| S3[Proceed to Ligation Setup]
    S2 -->|Blunt Blunt| S4[Add PEG and Increase Incubation Time]
    S2 -->|Incompatible| S5[Use Linker Adaptor or Blunt End After Fill in]
    S3 --> S6{Vector Phosphatase Treated}
    S6 -->|Yes CIP SAP| S7[Use 3 to 5 Fold Molar Excess Insert]
    S6 -->|No Untreated| S8[Use 1 to 1 Molar Ratio]
    S7 --> S9[Add T4 DNA Ligase and ATP]
    S8 --> S9
    S4 --> S9
    S5 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Incubate 16 C Overnight]
    S10 --> S11[Heat Inactivate 65 C 10 min]
    S11 --> S12[Transform Competent E coli]
    S12 --> S13[Plate on Selective Agar]
    S13 --> S14[Screen Colonies for Recombinant Plasmid]
```

### 4.3 Three-Step Catalytic Cycle of T4 DNA Ligase

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Free T4 Ligase plus ATP] --> A2[Step 1 Adenylation of Ligase Lys AMP plus PPi]
    A2 --> A3[Step 2 AMP Transfer to 5 Prime PO4 of DNA]
    A3 --> A4[Step 3 Sealing by 3 Prime OH Attack Phosphodiester Bond Formed]
    A4 --> A5[Free Ligase plus AMP Released]
    A5 --> A1
```

### 4.4 Diagram Caption Summary

- **Figure 1:** Functional block architecture of the ligation reaction showing all biochemical components, the three-step catalytic cycle, and the transition from linear fragments to covalently closed recombinant DNA suitable for transformation.
- **Figure 2:** A practical decision tree guiding the student from insert/vector preparation through to colony screening, with branch points for end compatibility and phosphatase treatment.
- **Figure 3:** The T4 DNA ligase catalytic cycle in three sub-steps, emphasizing the central role of ATP and the release of pyrophosphate / AMP.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q1.** What is DNA ligation? Name the enzyme used in recombinant DNA technology for ligation and state its co-factor.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> DNA ligation is the enzymatic joining of two DNA molecules (insert and vector) to form a single covalently closed recombinant DNA molecule by forming phosphodiester bonds between adjacent 3′-OH and 5′-PO₄ groups. The enzyme used is **T4 DNA ligase**, and its co-factor is **ATP (Adenosine Triphosphate)**, which is hydrolysed to AMP and PPi during the catalytic cycle. *(3 marks: definition 1 + enzyme 1 + co-factor 1)*

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
>
> **Q2.** Differentiate between cohesive end ligation and blunt end ligation.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Feature | Cohesive End Ligation | Blunt End Ligation |
> |---|---|---|
> | Overhangs | 4-nt single-stranded overhangs | None — fully base-paired |
> | Efficiency | High | Low (~10–100× slower) |
> | Conditions | 16 °C overnight or 25 °C, 30 min | 16 °C overnight + 5–10% PEG |
> | Enzyme units | 1 U | 3–5 U (more enzyme) |
> | Application | Routine restriction-based cloning | PCR blunt cloning, linker ligation |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the mechanism of T4 DNA ligase. Add a note on the co-factor requirement and explain why T4 ligase is preferred over *E. coli* ligase in rDNA technology.

**Model Answer (Step-by-step, 7 marks):**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** T4 DNA ligase, encoded by bacteriophage T4 gene 30, catalyses the formation of phosphodiester bonds between adjacent 3′-OH and 5′-PO₄ groups in dsDNA. It requires **ATP** as a co-factor (1 mM final) and Mg²⁺ ions.
2. **Step 1 — Adenylation of ligase (2 marks):** The ε-amino group of a conserved Lysine residue in the ligase attacks the α-phosphate of ATP, releasing pyrophosphate (PPi) and forming a **covalent ligase-Lys-AMP intermediate** (lysyl-adenylate). This "activated" ligase stores the energy needed for DNA joining.
3. **Step 2 — Adenylation of 5′-PO₄ of DNA (2 marks):** The AMP moiety is transferred from ligase-Lys to the free 5′-PO₄ of the DNA nick, creating **DNA-5′-adenylate** (a 5′ → 5′ phosphoanhydride). This activates the 5′ end for nucleophilic attack.
4. **Step 3 — Phosphodiester bond formation (1 mark):** The free 3′-OH attacks the activated 5′-PO₄, displacing AMP and forming the **phosphodiester bond**. The ligase is regenerated and free to begin another cycle.
5. **T4 vs E. coli ligase (1 mark):** T4 ligase uses ATP and ligates sticky, blunt, and nicked DNA — universal. *E. coli* ligase uses NAD⁺ and only efficiently seals **nicks** in dsDNA; it cannot ligate blunt or cohesive ends — hence T4 is preferred for cloning.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 4,800 bp plasmid is linearised, and 200 ng of it is to be ligated with a 1,600 bp insert at a 3:1 molar ratio. Calculate (i) the mass of insert required, and (ii) the pmol of vector ends. Given: 660 Da/bp for dsDNA.

**Model Answer (Step-by-step, 7 marks):**

**(i) Mass of insert (4 marks):**

Step 1 — Apply Formula 4:

$$m_{\text{insert}} = m_{\text{vector}} \times \frac{N_{\text{insert}}}{N_{\text{vector}}} \times R$$

Step 2 — Substitute:

$$m_{\text{insert}} = 200 \times \frac{1600}{4800} \times 3 = 200 \times 0.3333 \times 3 = 200 \text{ ng}$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{insert}} = 200 \text{ ng}}$$

Step 3 — Interpretation (1 mark): Hence, 200 ng of the 1.6 kb insert must be mixed with 200 ng of the 4.8 kb vector to achieve a 3:1 molar ratio.

**(ii) pmol of vector ends (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Compute MW of vector dsDNA:

$$M = 4800 \times 660 = 3,168,000 \text{ g/mol} = 3.168 \times 10^6 \text{ Da}$$

Step 2 — Apply Formula 3:

$$n_{\text{pmol}} = \frac{200}{4800 \times 0.66} = \frac{200}{3168} = 0.0631 \text{ pmol}$$

$$\boxed{n_{\text{vector}} \approx 0.063 \text{ pmol}}$$

Step 3 — At 3:1 ratio, pmol of insert required = 0.063 × 3 = **0.189 pmol**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the role of ATP, Mg²⁺, DTT, and BSA in a T4 DNA ligation reaction. Briefly discuss why a 1:3 vector:insert molar ratio is preferred for sticky-end ligation.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **ATP (2 marks):** ATP is the **obligate co-factor** that powers the ligase catalytic cycle. It is consumed in Step 1 (adenylation of ligase) and Step 2 (adenylation of DNA-5′-PO₄). One ATP is hydrolysed to AMP + PPi **per phosphodiester bond** formed. The supplied buffer (10× T4 ligase buffer) contains 1 mM ATP — sufficient for ~50–100 ligations per 20 µL reaction.
2. **Mg²⁺ (2 marks):** Mg²⁺ is the essential **divalent cation** that coordinates ATP (Mg-ATP is the true substrate) and stabilises the transition state during the nucleotidyl transfer reactions. Without Mg²⁺, ligase has negligible activity.
3. **DTT (1 mark):** DTT (Dithiothreitol, 1–10 mM) is a reducing agent that **prevents oxidation of the critical Cysteine and Lysine residues** in the ligase active site, keeping the enzyme catalytically active throughout the incubation.
4. **BSA (1 mark):** BSA (0.1–0.5 mg/mL) is a stabilising protein that **prevents surface adsorption of the ligase** to plastic tubes and pipette tips, and it suppresses non-specific nuclease activity.
5. **Why 1:3 ratio for sticky ends (1 mark):** A 3:1 insert:vector ratio **drives the equilibrium toward intermolecular ligation** (vector + insert) rather than vector self-ligation (vector + vector). Statistical collision frequency favours insert incorporation when insert is in molar excess.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the procedure to set up a 20 µL ligation reaction for cloning a 500 bp PCR product into a 3,000 bp vector (pUC19) at 3:1 molar ratio, given 50 ng/µL vector stock.

**Model Answer (Step-by-step procedure, 7 marks):**

1. **Step 1 — Calculate vector mass (1 mark):** Use 1 µL of 50 ng/µL vector stock → **50 ng of vector** in the reaction.
2. **Step 2 — Calculate insert mass (2 marks):** Apply Formula 4:

$$m_{\text{insert}} = 50 \times \frac{500}{3000} \times 3 = 50 \times 0.1667 \times 3 = 25 \text{ ng}$$

3. **Step 3 — Calculate insert volume (1 mark):** Assuming insert stock is 25 ng/µL → volume = 25 ng / 25 ng/µL = **1 µL**.
4. **Step 4 — Mix components on ice (1 mark):** Add 2 µL 10× T4 ligase buffer + 1 µL vector + 1 µL insert + 1 µL T4 DNA ligase (1 U) + nuclease-free water to 20 µL.
5. **Step 5 — Incubation (1 mark):** Mix gently, pulse-spin, incubate at **16 °C overnight** OR 25 °C for 30 min (rapid ligation). For blunt ends, 16 °C overnight + 5% PEG.
6. **Step 6 — Heat inactivation (1 mark):** 65 °C for 10 min. Store at –20 °C or proceed directly to transformation into competent *E. coli* (DH5α or TOP10).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in Ligation Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the co-factor** (ATP for T4 / NAD⁺ for E. coli) — loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing sticky-end and blunt-end conditions** — students often state 25 °C for blunt ligation; correct is 16 °C overnight + PEG.
> 3. **Using the wrong enzyme (E. coli ligase for cloning)** — E. coli ligase does NOT ligate sticky/blunt ends efficiently. T4 DNA ligase is mandatory.
> 4. **Inverting the vector:insert ratio** — the *insert* should be in molar *excess* (3×), not the vector.
> 5. **Skipping heat-inactivation step** before transformation — un-inactivated ligase reduces transformation efficiency by ~30–50%.
> 6. **Calculation errors in Formula 4** — confusing ng and pmol units, or using 0.66 vs 660 incorrectly.
> 7. **Ignoring the dephosphorylation step** — when the vector is not dephosphorylated, the student must justify the use of a 1:1 ratio and not 3:1 (or they lose 1 mark for a "practical" question).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DNA Ligation of Isolated Genes**
>
> - **Definition:** Covalent joining of 3′-OH and 5′-PO₄ via phosphodiester bond, catalysed by **T4 DNA ligase**.
> - **Co-factor:** **ATP** (1 mM final); consumed as **AMP + PPi** per bond.
> - **Energy currency:** 1 ATP = 1 phosphodiester bond = 1 nick sealed.
> - **Enzymes compared:** T4 (ATP, sticky/blunt/nicked — universal) vs E. coli (NAD⁺, nicks only).
> - **Three-step mechanism:** (i) Ligase-adenylation → (ii) DNA-adenylation → (iii) Phosphodiester bond formation with AMP release.
> - **Temperature:** **16 °C overnight** (sticky); **16 °C overnight + 5% PEG** (blunt); **25 °C, 30 min** (rapid sticky).
> - **Buffer components:** Tris-HCl pH 7.5–8.0, MgCl₂, DTT, ATP, BSA.
> - **Standard ratio:** **1 vector : 3 insert** (sticky) and **1 vector : 5–10 insert** (blunt).
> - **Calculations to master:**
>   - $M_{\text{dsDNA}} = N \times 660$ Da
>   - $n_{\text{pmol}} = \dfrac{\text{ng}}{N \times 0.66}$
>   - $m_{\text{insert}} = m_{\text{vector}} \times \dfrac{N_{\text{insert}}}{N_{\text{vector}}} \times R$
> - **Dephosphorylation:** CIP/SAP on vector → prevents self-ligation, increases insert uptake.
> - **Heat inactivation:** 65 °C, 10 min — mandatory before bacterial transformation.
> - **Troubleshooting quick-fixes:** Low colonies → check ATP freshness, ratio, gel-purify insert, use fresh ligase.
> - **Real-world uses:** Recombinant insulin, hepatitis B vaccine, Bt cotton, golden rice, mRNA/DNA vaccine backbones, CRISPR constructs.
> - **Board mantra:** *"Ligase welds, ligase needs ATP, ligase is T4, ligase likes 16 °C."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Restriction Mapping of Plasmids/Vectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL407 - MOLECULAR BIOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Restriction Mapping of Plasmids/Vectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Restriction Mapping** is an experimental procedure used to determine the precise linear order and physical distances (in base pairs, **bp** or kilobase pairs, **kb**) between the cleavage sites of **Type II restriction endonucleases** on a circular plasmid or linear vector DNA molecule. It relies on controlled enzymatic digestion and fragment size resolution by **agarose gel electrophoresis**.

### 1.1 The Working Vocabulary (KTU Terminology)

| Term | KTU Board Definition |
|------|----------------------|
| **Restriction Enzyme** | A bacterial endonuclease that recognizes a specific, short DNA sequence (4–8 bp) and cleaves at or near that site. |
| **Recognition Sequence** | A short, double-stranded DNA motif, usually a palindrome, that is the unique substrate for a particular enzyme. |
| **Palindromic Sequence** | Reads the same 5′→3′ on both complementary strands (e.g., GAATTC for EcoRI). |
| **Plasmid** | A small, circular, double-stranded DNA molecule (~1–200 kb) that replicates autonomously in bacterial cells. |
| **Vector** | A DNA carrier (plasmid, phage, cosmid) used to propagate a foreign gene. |
| **Single Digest** | Reaction containing DNA + **one** restriction enzyme. |
| **Double Digest** | Reaction containing DNA + **two** restriction enzymes simultaneously (in a compatible buffer). |
| **Ladder / Marker** | A mixture of DNA fragments of known sizes used as a size reference. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Favourite**: *EcoRI* (5′-G↓AATTC-3′), *HindIII* (5′-A↓AGCTT-3′), *BamHI* (5′-G↓GATCC-3′), and *PstI* (5′-CTGCA↓G-3′) are the four classical enzymes you **must memorize** for KTU exams.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Knotted Rope Model

Imagine you are handed a **circular rope (the plasmid)** whose total length is **5 m**, and somewhere along it are **invisible knots**. Each knot has a *unique hand-tied pattern*. You possess **three special scissors**:

- **Scissor-A** only cuts at Pattern-A knots.
- **Scissor-B** only cuts at Pattern-B knots.
- **Scissor-C** only cuts at Pattern-C knots.

When you cut the rope, the pieces fall apart and you can **measure their lengths with a measuring tape (the agarose gel)**.

- Cutting with **Scissor-A alone** tells you the distances between Pattern-A knots.
- Cutting with **Scissor-B alone** tells you the distances between Pattern-B knots.
- Cutting with **both A and B together** is like a "joint cutting test" — by comparing the **overlap of the pieces**, you can deduce the exact *sequence in which the knots appear along the rope*.

This is exactly what restriction mapping achieves — it converts **invisible genetic landmarks (enzyme sites)** into a **measurable, ordered physical map**.

### 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in Engineering Biology

| Real-World Application | Role of Restriction Map |
|------------------------|-------------------------|
| Recombinant DNA Technology | Identifies compatible cloning sites for foreign gene insertion. |
| Gene Therapy Vectors | Confirms structural integrity of engineered viral/plasmid vectors. |
| Industrial Enzyme Production | Validates insertion of cellulase/lipase genes into *E. coli* expression plasmids. |
| Forensic DNA Fingerprinting | Underpins RFLP-based identity analysis. |
| CRISPR-Cas Construct Design | Confirms absence/presence of off-target disruption sites. |
| Biosensor Development | Ensures correct orientation of reporter gene cassettes. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Six Sequential Pillars

The experiment is a **deductive puzzle** that proceeds through six rigorously ordered stages:

1. **Plasmid Isolation & Purification**
   - Lyse host bacterial cells (alkaline lysis method).
   - Remove genomic DNA, RNA, and proteins.
   - Precipitate pure plasmid DNA using ethanol/isopropanol.
   - Quantify purity via **A260/A280 ≈ 1.8** (KTU standard).

2. **Single Restriction Digestion**
   - Set up **3 reaction tubes**:
     - Tube-1: Plasmid + Enzyme-A (e.g., EcoRI)
     - Tube-2: Plasmid + Enzyme-B (e.g., HindIII)
     - Tube-3: Plasmid + **no enzyme** (control for uncut plasmid)
   - Incubate at **37 °C** for **1–2 hours** in a compatible buffer.

3. **Double Restriction Digestion**
   - Tube-4: Plasmid + Enzyme-A + Enzyme-B (simultaneously).
   - Validate buffer compatibility (e.g., BamHI + EcoRI work in a common buffer like **Buffer 3.1** at 37 °C).

4. **Agarose Gel Electrophoresis**
   - Cast a **0.8 % – 1.0 % agarose gel** in **1X TAE** or **1X TBE** buffer.
   - Load digest products alongside a **DNA ladder** (e.g., 1 kb or 100 bp ladder).
   - Run at **80–100 V** for 45–60 minutes.

5. **Fragment Size Estimation**
   - Visualize bands under **UV transilluminator** after ethidium bromide (EtBr) staining.
   - Measure migration distance of each band.
   - Plot **log(MW)** vs **migration distance** for the ladder → generate a standard curve.
   - Interpolate to find sizes of unknown fragments.

6. **Map Deduction**
   - Apply the **fragment-overlap logic** (Section 3) to assemble the cut-site map.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Is Necessary — The "Why" Behind the Logic

| Step | The "Why" Reasoning |
|------|---------------------|
| Single Digest | Establishes the **fragment set per enzyme**, giving distance information between identical sites. |
| Double Digest | Produces **shared fragments**, whose overlap is the *only* way to know which side of an A-site a B-site lies on. |
| Uncut Control | Confirms plasmid integrity; a smear means nuclease contamination. |
| DNA Ladder | Provides **absolute size calibration**; without it, sizes are unknown. |
| Agarose Gel | Resolves fragments down to ~100 bp resolution at 1 % gel. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table verbatim for ESE & CE-style questions.**

| # | Concept | Mathematical Rule / Formula | Engineering Significance |
|---|---------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Fragments from linear DNA | $n_{\text{fragments}} = n_{\text{sites}} + 1$ | Each cut adds one extra piece. |
| 2 | Fragments from circular DNA | $n_{\text{fragments}} = n_{\text{sites}}$ | One less than linear because the molecule is already closed. |
| 3 | Mass conservation (single digest) | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i = L_{\text{total}}$ | Sum of fragment sizes equals total plasmid length. |
| 4 | Mass conservation (double digest) | $\sum_{i} f_i^{AB} = \sum_{i} f_i^{A} = \sum_{i} f_i^{B}$ | The double-digest fragments must sum to the same total. |
| 5 | Migration-distance relation | $\log_{10}(MW) = -m \cdot d + c$ | $m$ = slope, $d$ = migration distance, $c$ = intercept (from ladder). |
| 6 | Size of unknown fragment | $MW_{\text{unk}} = 10^{(-m \cdot d_{\text{unk}} + c)}$ | Read off calibration curve. |
| 7 | Expected sites for an $n$-bp cutter | $E(\text{sites}) \approx \dfrac{L}{4^{n}}$ | Statistical estimate for random DNA (e.g., 6-bp cutter on 10 kb → ~2.4 sites). |
| 8 | Resolution of agarose gel | $R_{\min} \approx 0.1 \cdot L_{\text{target}}$ | Smaller fragments need higher % agarose (2–3 %). |
| 9 | Vector size (typical) | pBR322 = **4361 bp**; pUC19 = **2686 bp**; λ-DNA = **48,502 bp** | Most-used KTU reference molecules. |
| 10 | A260/A280 purity ratio | Pure DNA = **1.8 – 2.0**; Pure RNA ≈ 2.0; Protein contamination < 1.8 | Quality check before digestion. |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility Snapshot

- **Biopharmaceutical Engineering**: Mapping of expression plasmids (e.g., pET-28a) used to produce human insulin in *E. coli* confirms gene orientation and absence of unwanted mutations after cloning.
