topics.title: Western Blotting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL407 - MOLECULAR BIOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Western Blotting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Western Blotting (Immunoblotting)** is an analytical immunodetection technique used to identify and quantify specific proteins from a complex mixture. The method, first described by W. Neal Burnette in 1981 (named after the analogous Southern blot for DNA), combines the high-resolution size separation capability of **SDS-PAGE** with the high specificity of **antibody-antigen recognition**.

The principle relies on three sequential biochemical events:

1. **Electrophoretic Size Separation** — Denatured proteins migrate through a polyacrylamide gel matrix under an applied electric field, separating strictly according to their molecular weight.
2. **Electroelution Transfer** — The resolved protein bands are horizontally transferred (blotted) onto a robust solid support membrane (nitrocellulose or PVDF), preserving the spatial separation pattern.
3. **Immunochemical Detection** — Target proteins (antigens) are probed sequentially with a **primary antibody** (specific to the antigen) and a labeled **secondary antibody** (specific to the primary antibody), enabling visualization.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCBBL407 / Module Experiment 2):** Students are expected to perform Western Blotting, identify a target protein (commonly **GFP**, **GFPuv**, **BSA**, or **Insulin**), interpret molecular weight markers, and troubleshoot issues like high background, weak signal, or smeared bands.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Missing Person in a Stadium"

Imagine a packed cricket stadium with **50,000 people** (the cell lysate containing thousands of different proteins). You are searching for **one specific individual** (your target protein, say GFP of ~27 kDa).

- **Step 1 — Stadium Aisles:** You first sort the crowd by height using numbered corridors (SDS-PAGE gel). Shorter people exit through lane 1, taller through lane 10. Now you know *which row* your target sits in based on his height.
- **Step 2 — Photographic Copy:** You photograph the entire aisle arrangement and paste a perfect copy onto a paper sheet (transfer to nitrocellulose membrane). The original gel is fragile and cannot be reused; the membrane copy is stable.
- **Step 3 — Sniffer Dog (Primary Antibody):** You release a sniffer dog trained to detect *only* your missing person's unique scent. The dog sits down next to the right person (binds the antigen).
- **Step 4 — Spotlight on the Dog (Secondary Antibody):** A second handler shines a flashlight at the dog (HRP- or AP-conjugated secondary antibody). Now you can see *exactly where* your person is sitting, even in the dark stadium (chemiluminescent/colourimetric detection).

This is, in essence, Western Blotting — **specific detection in a complex mixture using a layered antibody recognition system.**

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology — Quick Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **SDS-PAGE** | Sodium Dodecyl Sulphate–Polyacrylamide Gel Electrophoresis; denaturing technique separating proteins by MW |
| **PVDF** | Polyvinylidene Difluoride membrane; high binding capacity, hydrophobic |
| **NC** | Nitrocellulose membrane; classical choice, less robust than PVDF |
| **Primary Antibody (1° Ab)** | An immunoglobulin that specifically binds the target antigen epitope |
| **Secondary Antibody (2° Ab)** | An immunoglobulin conjugated to a reporter (HRP/AP) that binds the 1° Ab |
| **Blocking** | Pre-saturation of membrane with inert protein (5% non-fat milk or BSA) to prevent non-specific binding |
| **TBST** | Tris-Buffered Saline + 0.1% Tween-20; washing buffer |
| **HRP** | Horseradish Peroxidase; enzyme catalyzing chemiluminescent substrates (ECL) |
| **AP** | Alkaline Phosphatase; enzyme producing coloured/fluorescent products (BCIP/NBT) |
| **ECL** | Enhanced Chemiluminescence; HRP + luminol + H₂O₂ reaction emitting light |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (frequently asked in KTU exams):**
> - **Western Blot** detects **PROTEINS** using antibodies.
> - **Southern Blot** detects **DNA** using labelled nucleic acid probes.
> - **Northern Blot** detects **RNA** using labelled nucleic acid probes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow — Twelve Sequential Logic Steps

### **PHASE A — Sample Preparation & Size Separation**

**Step 1 — Cell Lysis & Protein Extraction**
Tissue or cells are homogenized in **RIPA buffer** (Radio-Immunoprecipitation Assay buffer) containing detergents (1% NP-40, 0.5% sodium deoxycholate, 0.1% SDS), protease inhibitors (PMSF, leupeptin, aprotinin), and phosphatase inhibitors. Mechanical disruption (sonication/bead-beating) is followed by centrifugation at **12,000 × g, 4 °C, 20 min** to recover soluble proteins in the supernatant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why protease inhibitors?** Once cells are lysed, endogenous proteases (cathepsins, calpains) are released and begin digesting proteins. PMSF irreversibly inhibits serine proteases; without it, your target protein may be degraded before detection.

**Step 2 — Protein Quantification**
The clear lysate is quantified using the **Bradford Assay**, **BCA Assay**, or **Lowry Assay**. Typical working concentration is **1–5 mg/mL**.

**Step 3 — Denaturation in Laemmli Buffer**
Proteins are boiled at **95 °C for 5–10 min** in **2× SDS sample buffer** containing:

- **SDS (2% w/v)** — anionic detergent; unfolds proteins and confers uniform negative charge per unit mass (1.4 g SDS per g protein).
- **β-Mercaptoethanol (β-ME, 5% v/v) or DTT (50 mM)** — reducing agent; cleaves intra/inter-chain disulfide bonds.
- **Glycerol (10%)** — increases sample density for gel loading.
- **Bromophenol Blue (0.02%)** — tracking dye.
- **Tris-HCl (pH 6.8, 62.5 mM)** — buffering.

**Step 4 — SDS-PAGE Electrophoresis**
Proteins are loaded onto a polyacrylamide gel (stacking gel pH 6.8 over resolving gel pH 8.8) and run at **80 V (stacking) → 120–150 V (resolving)** in **Tris-Glycine-SDS running buffer (pH 8.3)**. Migration follows the empirical relation:

$$\log(MW) = -k \cdot R_f + b$$

where $R_f$ is the relative mobility (distance migrated by protein ÷ distance migrated by dye front), $k$ is the slope, and $b$ is the intercept — both derived from the standard curve.

### **PHASE B — Transfer to Solid Support**

**Step 5 — Membrane Activation & Gel Equilibration**
- **PVDF:** Pre-activated in **100% methanol for 30 s** (critical for hydrophilicity).
- **Nitrocellulose:** Equilibrated directly in transfer buffer.
- Gel is equilibrated in **transfer buffer** for 15 min to remove residual SDS that would impede binding.

**Step 6 — Electrotransfer**
Two common methods exist:

| Method | Buffer System | Time | Field | Use Case |
|--------|---------------|------|-------|----------|
| **Wet/Tank Transfer** | Tris-Glycine ± 20% methanol | 1–2 h (or overnight at 30 V) | 100 V constant | Large proteins >100 kDa |
| **Semi-Dry Transfer** | Tris-Glycine (continuous buffer) | 30–60 min | 10–15 V | Routine, fast, small-medium proteins |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Methanol improves binding of small proteins but **reduces transfer efficiency of large proteins** (>100 kDa) by fixing them in the gel. For large proteins, use **SDS-supplemented transfer buffer** or prolonged transfer.

### **PHASE C — Blocking & Immunodetection**

**Step 7 — Blocking**
Membrane incubated in **5% non-fat dry milk (NFDM) in TBST** or **3–5% BSA in TBST** for **1 h at room temperature (RT)** or overnight at 4 °C. Milk proteins occupy unbound hydrophobic sites, preventing antibody non-specific binding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule:** Never use milk as a blocker when probing for **phosphorylated proteins** — milk contains casein, a phosphoprotein that cross-reacts with anti-phospho antibodies. Use **BSA** instead.

**Step 8 — Primary Antibody (1° Ab) Incubation**
Membrane incubated with 1° antibody diluted (typically **1:500 to 1:5000** in blocking buffer) for **1–2 h RT or overnight at 4 °C** with gentle rocking. The antibody binds its specific epitope on the target protein.

**Step 9 — Washing**
Membrane washed **3 × 5 min** in **TBST** to remove unbound 1° Ab. Inadequate washing is the #1 cause of high background.

**Step 10 — Secondary Antibody (2° Ab) Incubation**
HRP- or AP-conjugated 2° antibody (typically **1:2000 to 1:10000** in blocking buffer) added for **1 h at RT**. The 2° Ab recognizes the constant (Fc) region of the 1° Ab.

**Step 11 — Final Washing**
Membrane washed **3 × 5 min** in TBST, then once in TBS (no Tween) to remove detergent that may interfere with detection.

**Step 12 — Detection & Imaging**
- **Chemiluminescent (ECL):** HRP + luminol + H₂O₂ → oxidized luminol emits light at **428 nm**; captured on X-ray film or CCD imager.
- **Colourimetric:** AP + BCIP/NBT → purple insoluble precipitate at the antigen site.
- **Fluorescent:** IRDye/Cy-conjugated 2° Ab; detected on Li-Cor Odyssey system.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Quantitative Equations

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $R_f = \dfrac{d_{protein}}{d_{dye\ front}}$ | $R_f$ dimensionless; $d$ in cm | Relative mobility calculation |
| 2 | $\log(MW) = m \cdot R_f + c$ | $MW$ in Daltons (Da); $R_f$ dimensionless | Standard curve for MW determination |
| 3 | $C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2$ | $C$ concentration; $V$ volume | Antibody dilution calculation |
| 4 | $MW_{native} = MW_{subunit} \times n$ | $n$ = number of subunits | Multimeric protein prediction |
| 5 | $\%\,Acrylamide \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{MW}}$ | Empirical | Gel percentage selection |
| 6 | $I_{band} \propto [Protein]$ | $I$ band intensity (densitometry) | Semi-quantitative analysis |
| 7 | $E = \dfrac{V}{d}$ | $E$ electric field (V/cm); $d$ electrode distance | Electrophoresis voltage gradient |
| 8 | $Power = V \times I$ | Power in Watts | Heat dissipation during transfer |
| 9 | $Resolution \approx \sqrt{L \cdot V \cdot t}$ | $L$ gel length; $V$ voltage; $t$ time | SDS-PAGE resolution optimization |
| 10 | $Yield(\%) = \dfrac{I_{sample}}{I_{reference}} \times 100$ | $I$ integrated band density | Normalization to loading control |

### **Recommended Gel Percentages for Different MW Ranges**

| Protein MW Range (kDa) | Optimal % Acrylamide | Application Examples |
|------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **10 – 30** | **15%** | Insulin (5.8 kDa), GFP (27 kDa) |
| **30 – 60** | **12%** | BSA (66 kDa), GAPDH (37 kDa) |
| **60 – 120** | **10%** | β-galactosidase (116 kDa) |
| **>120** | **7–8%** | Myosin heavy chain (220 kDa) |

> [!NOTE]
> **GFP (Green Fluorescent Protein), the most commonly probed protein in KTU lab exams, is 27 kDa (236 amino acids, 26.9 kDa theoretical).** A 12% resolving gel is recommended for clear separation.

### **Standard Transfer Buffer (Towbin Buffer, 1 L, pH 8.3)**

$$\text{Tris-base} = 25\,mM = 3.03\,g$$

$$\text{Glycine} = 192\,mM = 14.4\,g$$

$$\text{Methanol (optional)} = 20\% = 200\,mL$$

$$\text{ddH}_2O \to 1000\,mL$$

### **TBST (Washing Buffer, 1 L, pH 7.6)**

$$\text{Tris-HCl (pH 7.6)} = 20\,mM$$

$$\text{NaCl} = 150\,mM$$

$$\text{Tween-20} = 0.1\%\ (v/v)$$

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Western Blotting

- **Clinical Diagnostics:** Confirmatory test for **HIV** (anti-p24, gp120/gp41), **Lyme disease**, **Hepatitis B** (HBsAg), and prion disorders (PrPˢᶜ in BSE).
- **Pharmaceutical QC:** Validating recombinant protein expression (insulin, erythropoietin, monoclonal antibodies).
- **Disease Research:** Differential expression of oncoproteins (p53, HER2), neurodegeneration markers (α-synuclein, tau).
- **Gene-Expression Confirmation:** Validates results obtained from qPCR/microarray at the protein level.
- **Phospho-Protein Studies:** Maps signalling cascades (MAPK, PI3K/Akt) by detecting phosphorylation-specific epitopes.
- **COVID-19 Research:** Spike protein detection in vaccine characterization and serological assays.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Molecular Weight Determination from Standard Curve

### **Problem Context**
During a KTU lab session, a student runs a 12% SDS-PAGE alongside a pre-stained protein ladder with the following data:

| Lane | Protein | Distance Migrated (cm) |
|------|---------|------------------------|
| L1 | Ladder — 250 kDa | 0.6 |
| L2 | Ladder — 150 kDa | 1.0 |
| L3 | Ladder — 100 kDa | 1.4 |
| L4 | Ladder — 75 kDa | 1.9 |
| L5 | Ladder — 50 kDa | 2.7 |
| L6 | Ladder — 37 kDa | 3.5 |
| L7 | Ladder — 25 kDa | 4.6 |
| L8 | Ladder — 20 kDa | 5.2 |
| L9 | Ladder — 15 kDa | 5.9 |
| L10 | **Dye front** | 7.0 |
| S1 | **Unknown protein** | 2.5 |

### **Step 1 — Compute Relative Mobility ($R_f$) for each ladder band**

The dye front migrated **7.0 cm**. We divide each band's migration by the dye front distance.

$$R_f^{(250)} = \frac{0.6}{7.0} = 0.0857$$

$$R_f^{(150)} = \frac{1.0}{7.0} = 0.1429$$

$$R_f^{(100)} = \frac{1.4}{7.0} = 0.2000$$

$$R_f^{(75)} = \frac{1.9}{7.0} = 0.2714$$

$$R_f^{(50)} = \frac{2.7}{7.0} = 0.3857$$

$$R_f^{(37)} = \frac{3.5}{7.0} = 0.5000$$

$$R_f^{(25)} = \frac{4.6}{7.0} = 0.6571$$

$$R_f^{(20)} = \frac{5.2}{7.0} = 0.7429$$

$$R_f^{(15)} = \frac{5.9}{7.0} = 0.8429$$

**Textual explanation:** $R_f$ normalizes each band's migration distance to a fraction of the total run. It ranges from 0 (band never left well) to 1 (band migrated with dye front). This makes it independent of total run time or gel length.

### **Step 2 — Compute log(MW) for each band**

| Band MW (Da) | $\log_{10}(MW)$ |
|--------------|------------------|
| 250,000 | **5.3979** |
| 150,000 | **5.1761** |
| 100,000 | **5.0000** |
| 75,000 | **4.8751** |
| 50,000 | **4.6990** |
| 37,000 | **4.5682** |
| 25,000 | **4.3979** |
| 20,000 | **4.3010** |
| 15,000 | **4.1761** |

### **Step 3 — Linear Regression: $\log(MW)$ vs $R_f$**

Using the method of least squares, we obtain the standard equation:

$$\log(MW) = m \cdot R_f + c$$

Applying linear regression on the 9 ladder points:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Slope } (m) &= \frac{N \sum(xy) - \sum x \sum y}{N \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2} \\
\text{Intercept } (c) &= \frac{\sum y - m \sum x}{N}
\end{aligned}$$

With the computed values from the table above, the final regression line yields:

$$\log(MW) = -1.786 \cdot R_f + 5.554$$

### **Step 4 — Determine the MW of the unknown protein**

For the unknown band at $R_f^{(unk)} = 2.5/7.0 = 0.3571$:

$$\log(MW) = -1.786 \times 0.3571 + 5.554$$

$$\log(MW) = -0.6378 + 5.554 = 4.916$$

$$MW = 10^{4.916} = 82{,}414\ Da \approx 82.4\ kDa$$

**Interpretation:** The unknown protein has a molecular weight of approximately **82.4 kDa**, suggesting it could be **Transferrin (~80 kDa)** or a related glycoprotein.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Antibody Dilution Calculation

### **Problem Context**
A vial of anti-GFP primary antibody has a stock concentration of **1 mg/mL**. The vendor recommends a working dilution of **1:1000** in **10 mL** of blocking buffer. Calculate the volume of stock antibody required.

### **Step 1 — Apply the dilution formula $C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2$**

$$C_1 = \text{Stock conc.} = 1\,mg/mL$$

$$C_2 = \text{Working conc.} = \frac{1\,mg/mL}{1000} = 0.001\,mg/mL = 1\,\mu g/mL$$

$$V_2 = \text{Final volume} = 10\,mL$$

$$V_1 = \frac{C_2 \times V_2}{C_1} = \frac{0.001\,mg/mL \times 10\,mL}{1\,mg/mL}$$

$$V_1 = 0.01\,mL = 10\,\mu L$$

### **Step 2 — Working protocol**

$$\text{Take } 10\,\mu L \text{ of primary antibody} + 9990\,\mu L \text{ of blocking buffer} = 10\,mL \text{ of 1:1000 working solution}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Semi-Quantitative Densitometric Analysis

### **Problem Context**
A Western blot image (after ECL exposure) shows two bands: the target protein and a housekeeping loading control (GAPDH, 37 kDa). Densitometric analysis yields the following integrated band intensities:

| Sample | Target Protein (T) | GAPDH (G) | Ratio (T/G) |
|--------|---------------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Control** | 12,500 | 25,000 | 0.500 |
| **Treated A** | 18,000 | 24,500 | 0.735 |
| **Treated B** | 22,400 | 25,200 | 0.889 |

### **Step 1 — Normalization**

Normalization to a housekeeping protein (GAPDH, β-actin, α-tubulin) corrects for variations in sample loading and transfer efficiency. The normalized ratio is:

$$R_{normalized} = \frac{I_{target}}{I_{loading\ control}}$$

### **Step 2 — Fold-Change Calculation**

$$\text{Fold-change}_{A} = \frac{R_{A}}{R_{control}} = \frac{0.735}{0.500} = 1.47\text{-fold}$$

$$\text{Fold-change}_{B} = \frac{R_{B}}{R_{control}} = \frac{0.889}{0.500} = 1.78\text{-fold}$$

### **Step 3 — %Upregulation**

$$\%\,Upregulation_A = (1.47 - 1.00) \times 100 = 47\%$$

$$\%\,Upregulation_B = (1.78 - 1.00) \times 100 = 78\%$$

**Conclusion:** Treatment B induced a **78% increase** in target protein expression relative to control, while Treatment A induced a **47% increase**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Acrylamide % Selection for a Target MW

The empirical relation governing protein mobility through polyacrylamide gels is:

$$\log(MW) = -k \cdot \%T + const$$

where $\%T$ is the total acrylamide concentration. To maximize resolution near a target molecular weight $MW_{target}$, use the empirically validated table:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Gel \%} &= \dfrac{-(\log(MW_{target}) - 5.2)}{0.124} \quad \text{(for Tris-Glycine system)}
\end{aligned}$$

### **Example Calculation for GFP (27 kDa)**

$$\log(27{,}000) = 4.431$$

$$\text{Gel \%} = \frac{-(4.431 - 5.2)}{0.124} = \frac{0.769}{0.124} \approx 6.2\%$$

**Caveat:** This formula gives the theoretical optimum for maximum resolution. For routine use, a **12% gel** is the practical choice because it provides a useful separation window across 20–100 kDa, comfortably accommodating GFP (27 kDa) and any common loading control.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Troubleshooting Guide for Western Blot Failures

| Problem | Probable Cause | Corrective Action |
|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| **No signal (blank blot)** | Insufficient protein loaded | Load 20–50 μg total protein |
| | Primary antibody inactive | Use fresh aliquot; check expiry |
| | Incompatible blocking agent | Try BSA instead of milk (for phospho-Abs) |
| | Membrane dried out | Re-wet in methanol, never let dry |
| **High background (dark blot)** | Insufficient blocking | Increase blocker to 5–10%; extend to 2 h |
| | Insufficient washing | Increase to 5 × 10 min TBST washes |
| | 2° Ab concentration too high | Titrate down (1:5000 → 1:20000) |
| **Multiple non-specific bands** | Antibody cross-reactivity | Increase antibody dilution; use affinity-purified Ab |
| | Proteolysis during sample prep | Add fresh protease inhibitors |
| **Smeared bands** | Sample overloaded | Reduce protein load to 10–20 μg |
| | Gel run too fast | Reduce voltage; run at 100 V |
| **White (negative) bands on dark background** | ECL substrate exhaustion | Use fresh ECL; avoid excessive HRP |
| **Patchy / uneven background** | Insufficient agitation during incubation | Use rocker/shaker throughout |
| **Band at wrong MW** | Protein modification (glycosylation, cleavage) | Compare with predicted MW from sequence; deglycosylate |
| **Transfer inefficient (band still in gel)** | Transfer time insufficient | Increase to 2 h at 100 V (wet); check membrane orientation |

## 4.3 Mermaid Functional Architecture — Immunodetection Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Target Protein on Membrane] --> Q[Step A: Primary Antibody binds antigen epitope]
    Q --> R[Step B: Wash to remove unbound 1 Ab]

    S --> T[Step D: Wash to remove unbound 2 Ab]
    T --> U[Step E: Add Luminol and H2O2 substrates]
    U --> V[Step F: HRP catalyzes oxidation of luminol]
    V --> W[Step G: Oxidized luminol emits photons at 428 nm]
    W --> X[Step H: Photons captured by CCD imager or X-ray film]
```

## 4.4 Signal Amplification Logic — Layered Antibody Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Single Target Antigen on Membrane] --> B[Bound by 1 Primary Antibody]
    B --> C1[Bound by 1 Secondary Antibody HRP conjugated]
    B --> C2[Bound by 1 Secondary Antibody HRP conjugated]
    B --> C3[Bound by 1 Secondary Antibody HRP conjugated]
    C1 --> D1[Multiple HRP enzymes per 2 Ab]
    C2 --> D2[Multiple HRP enzymes per 2 Ab]
    C3 --> D3[Multiple HRP enzymes per 2 Ab]
    D1 --> E[Thousands of luminescent events per target molecule]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E

    style A fill:#ffe1e1
    style E fill:#e1ffe1
```

**Explanation of amplification:** A single target protein can bind **one** primary antibody, but each primary antibody has **two** identical Fc regions, each binding a secondary antibody. Each secondary antibody carries **2–4 HRP molecules**, each HRP catalyses **>1000 luminol oxidation events per second**. The cumulative amplification is therefore massive — a single antigen can yield a **10⁶–10⁸-fold signal amplification**, which is why Western blotting is exquisitely sensitive (detection limit: **femtogram levels, ~10⁻¹⁵ g**).
