# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: Define Southern hybridization. Name the scientist who developed this technique and state the principle on which it is based.**

**Model Answer:**

**Southern hybridization** (also called the **Southern blot technique**) is a molecular biology method used to detect a **specific DNA sequence** within a complex DNA sample. It was developed by **Edwin M. Southern** at the University of Edinburgh in **1975**.

**Principle:** The technique is based on the **Watson-Crick complementary base pairing** between the target ssDNA immobilized on a membrane and a **labeled single-stranded DNA or RNA probe**. The detection of the hybridized probe-target duplex is achieved by autoradiography (radioactive label) or chemiluminescence/colorimetry (non-radioactive labels).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is a hybridization probe? List any three methods used to label a DNA probe.**

**Model Answer:**

A **hybridization probe** is a short, single-stranded, **labeled DNA or RNA molecule** whose sequence is **complementary** to the target DNA sequence being detected. The label enables visualization after the probe hybridizes to the target on the membrane.

**Three methods of probe labeling:**

1. **Random Priming (Feinberg & Vogelstein, 1983)** — Uses random hexanucleotide primers and the Klenow fragment of *E. coli* DNA Polymerase I to incorporate labeled dNTPs (e.g., ³²P-dCTP or DIG-dUTP) into a denatured DNA template.

2. **Nick Translation** — Uses DNase I to introduce nicks in dsDNA, followed by DNA Polymerase I that simultaneously removes nucleotides from the 5′ side of the nick and synthesizes new DNA, incorporating labeled dNTPs.

3. **End-Labeling using T4 Polynucleotide Kinase (PNK)** — Transfers the γ-phosphate of γ-³²P-ATP to the 5′-OH end of a DNA probe. Primarily used for short oligonucleotide probes.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 2 long-answer questions carry 14 marks with sub-parts of 7 + 7. Internal choice is provided between two questions.**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - July 2024]

**Q: Describe the principle, procedure, and applications of Southern hybridization for the detection of a cloned DNA fragment. Add a note on probe labeling methods.**

#### Part (a) — Principle and Step-by-Step Procedure (7 Marks)

**Principle:**
Southern hybridization is founded on **complementary base pairing (A=T, G≡C)** between a **denatured, immobilized target DNA** and a **labeled single-stranded probe**. The double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) is first fragmented using **restriction endonucleases**, separated by size on an **agarose gel**, transferred (blotted) onto a **solid support membrane** (nitrocellulose/nylon), and detected by hybridization with a **sequence-specific labeled probe**.

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

**Step 1 — Restriction Digestion (1 mark):**
The recombinant plasmid DNA (or genomic DNA) is digested with a **restriction endonuclease** specific to the multiple cloning site (e.g., *Eco*RI). A typical reaction contains **1–2 µg DNA, 1× restriction buffer, 10 U enzyme, BSA**, and is incubated at **37 °C for 1–2 hours**. A molecular weight marker (λ/*Hin*dIII ladder) is digested in parallel.

**Step 2 — Agarose Gel Electrophoresis (1 mark):**
The digested DNA is loaded onto a **0.8–1.0% agarose gel** prepared in **1× TAE buffer with ethidium bromide (0.5 µg/mL)**. Electrophoresis is performed at **80 V for 1–2 hours**. The gel is visualized under **UV transillumination** and photographed for record.

**Step 3 — Denaturation and Neutralization (1 mark):**
The gel is immersed in **denaturation solution (1.5 M NaCl, 0.5 M NaOH)** for **30 minutes** at room temperature with gentle shaking. This converts dsDNA → ssDNA. The gel is then rinsed in distilled water and soaked in **neutralization solution (1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.4, 1.5 M NaCl)** for **30 minutes**.

**Step 4 — Capillary Transfer (1 mark):**
A **Southern blot transfer apparatus** is set up: a buffer reservoir containing **20× SSC**, a Whatman 3MM wick, the gel (DNA-side up), a **positively charged nylon membrane**, three Whatman 3MM sheets, a stack of dry paper towels, and a **500 g weight**. Transfer is allowed to proceed for **12–18 hours** (overnight) by capillary action.

**Step 5 — Fixation (1 mark):**
The membrane is removed and DNA is fixed by either:
- **UV crosslinking** at **120 mJ/cm²** for **30 seconds** (for nylon membranes), OR
- **Baking** at **80 °C for 2 hours** in a vacuum oven (for nitrocellulose).

**Step 6 — Pre-hybridization, Hybridization, and Washing (1 mark):**
The membrane is incubated in **pre-hybridization solution (6× SSC, 5× Denhardt's solution, 0.5% SDS, 100 µg/mL denatured salmon sperm DNA)** for **2–4 hours at 65 °C** to block non-specific binding sites. The **labeled, denatured probe** is added and hybridization proceeds for **16–24 hours at 65 °C** (or 42 °C with 50% formamide). The membrane is then washed sequentially:
- **Wash 1**: 2× SSC, 0.1% SDS at room temperature (low stringency)
- **Wash 2**: 0.1× SSC, 0.1% SDS at 65 °C (high stringency)

**Step 7 — Detection (1 mark):**
The hybridized probe is detected by:
- **Autoradiography**: Membrane exposed to X-ray film at **−80 °C with intensifying screens for 1–7 days** (for ³²P probes), OR
- **Chemiluminescence**: Anti-DIG-AP antibody + CSPD substrate + X-ray exposure for **15–60 minutes** (for DIG-labeled probes), OR
- **Colorimetry**: BCIP/NBT substrate yielding a **purple precipitate** on the membrane (for biotin/DIG probes).

**Result Interpretation:** A dark band at a specific position on the developed film/membrane corresponds to the **fragment of expected size** (e.g., 1.5 kb if the cloned insert is 1.5 kb), confirming the presence of the target cloned fragment in the sample.

---

#### Part (b) — Applications of Southern Hybridization & Probe Labeling Methods (7 Marks)

**Applications (3 marks):**

| Application | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| **RFLP Analysis** | Restriction Fragment Length Polymorphism for DNA fingerprinting, paternity testing, and forensic identification |
| **Gene Identification & Mapping** | Confirms the presence, size, and copy number of a cloned gene in a genomic DNA sample |
| **Diagnosis of Genetic Diseases** | Detects mutations causing sickle cell anemia, thalassemia, cystic fibrosis, etc. |
| **Detection of Gene Rearrangements** | Identifies chromosomal translocations in cancers (e.g., *bcr-abl* in CML) |
| **Confirmation of Transgenic Integration** | Verifies stable integration of transgenes in genetically modified organisms (GMOs) |
| **Detection of Viral DNA** | Identifies integration of viral DNA (e.g., HBV) into the host genome |
| **Phylogenetic Studies** | Compares homologous gene sequences across species |

**Probe Labeling Methods (4 marks):**

**1. Random Priming (Feinberg & Vogelstein, 1983) — 2 marks:**
- The most widely used method for labeling dsDNA probes.
- The template DNA is denatured by boiling.
- A mixture of **random hexanucleotide primers** is annealed to the template.
- The **Klenow fragment** (5′→3′ polymerase, lacking 5′→3′ exonuclease activity) extends the primers, incorporating **labeled dNTPs** (e.g., α-³²P-dCTP or DIG-dUTP).
- Yields probes with **very high specific activity (>10⁹ cpm/µg)**.
- Advantage: high specific activity, uniform labeling, works for any dsDNA.

**2. Nick Translation — 1 mark:**
- DNase I introduces random nicks in the dsDNA template.
- *E. coli* DNA Polymerase I extends from the 3′-OH at each nick while **removing nucleotides** with its 5′→3′ exonuclease activity.
- Labeled dNTPs are incorporated in place of the removed nucleotides.
- Result: labeled probe with **uniformly distributed label**.

**3. End-Labeling with T4 Polynucleotide Kinase (PNK) — 1 mark:**
- Transfers **γ-³²P from γ-³²P-ATP** to the 5′-OH of the probe.
- Used for **short oligonucleotide probes** (e.g., synthetic oligos for allele-specific oligotyping).
- Label is at **one end only** → lower specific activity, but useful for defined-length probes.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Alternative Choice]

**Q: Discuss in detail the concept of stringency in Southern hybridization. How are the factors affecting stringency controlled experimentally? Calculate the melting temperature (Tm) of a 250 bp probe with 48% GC content hybridized in 4× SSC buffer containing 40% formamide. Suggest a suitable hybridization temperature.**

#### Part (a) — Concept of Stringency and Experimental Control (7 Marks)

**Definition of Stringency (2 marks):**
**Stringency** refers to the experimental conditions that govern the **specificity** of probe-target hybridization. **High stringency** allows only perfectly matched duplexes to remain (high specificity, low sensitivity). **Low stringency** permits mismatched duplexes to remain (lower specificity, higher sensitivity for related sequences).

**Factors Affecting Stringency and Their Experimental Control (5 marks):**

**1. Temperature (1.5 marks):**
- Higher temperature → higher stringency.
- Controlled by **hybridization oven/water bath temperature** (typically **42 °C with formamide, 65 °C without**).
- High stringency post-hybridization washes are done at **65 °C in 0.1× SSC**.

**2. Salt Concentration (SSC) (1.5 marks):**
- **Na⁺ ions shield the negative phosphate backbone repulsion** between probe and target.
- **Lower salt = higher stringency** (less shielding = more discrimination against mismatches).
- Controlled by adjusting **SSC concentration** (e.g., 6× SSC for hybridization, 0.1× SSC for high-stringency wash).

**3. Formamide (1 mark):**
- A **hydrogen-bond destabilizing agent** that allows hybridization at lower temperatures.
- Standard concentration: **50% formamide** lowers effective hybridization T by **~30 °C**.
- Controlled by **direct addition to hybridization buffer** and is **omitted from wash buffer**.

**4. Denhardt's Solution and Blocking Agents (0.5 marks):**
- Reduces non-specific probe binding to the membrane.
- Contains **BSA, Ficoll 400, and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP)**.
- Higher concentration of blocking agents → less non-specific background.

**5. Probe Length and Mismatches (0.5 marks):**
- Longer probes tolerate more mismatches.
- Highly conserved regions are preferred for probe design.

---

#### Part (b) — Melting Temperature Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Probe length $N = 250$ bp
- GC content $= 48\%$
- Salt concentration: $4\times \text{SSC} = 4 \times 0.15 = 0.6$ M Na⁺
- Formamide concentration $= 40\%$

**Step 1 — Calculate $\log_{10}[\text{Na}^+]$ (1 mark):**

$$
\log_{10}(0.6) \;=\; -0.2218
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Tm equation with formamide correction (3 marks):**

$$
T_m \;=\; 81.5 \;+\; 16.6 \log_{10}[\text{Na}^+] \;+\; 0.41(\%GC) \;-\; \frac{600}{N} \;-\; 0.61(\% \text{ formamide})
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term (2 marks):**

| Term | Calculation | Value (°C) |
|------|-------------|------------|
| Constant | $81.5$ | $+81.50$ |
| Salt | $16.6 \times (-0.2218)$ | $-3.68$ |
| GC | $0.41 \times 48$ | $+19.68$ |
| Length | $600 / 250$ | $-2.40$ |
| Formamide | $0.61 \times 40$ | $-24.40$ |

**Step 4 — Sum and obtain Tm (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_m \;&=\; 81.50 \;-\; 3.68 \;+\; 19.68 \;-\; 2.40 \;-\; 24.40 \\
&=\; 101.18 \;-\; 30.48 \\
&=\; 70.70\ ^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Recommend Hybridization Temperature:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{hybridization}} \;&=\; T_m \;-\; 15\ ^\circ\text{C} \\
&=\; 70.70 \;-\; 15 \\
&=\; 55.70\ ^\circ\text{C} \;\approx\; 56\ ^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $T_m = 70.7\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and a suitable hybridization temperature is **56 °C**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks in Southern Hybridization Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Southern with Northern/Western** — Southern = DNA, Northern = RNA, Western = Protein. Examiners deduct 1 full mark for this mix-up.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert SSC to Molar Na⁺** — In Tm calculations, students often write "$6\times \text{SSC}$" in the formula instead of converting it to **$0.9$ M Na⁺**. This produces a wrong answer (~3–5 marks lost).
>
> 3. **Writing "Denaturation by Boiling"** — DNA on a Southern blot membrane is denatured using **NaOH**, NOT boiling. Boiling is used for probe denaturation, not for the membrane-bound DNA (the membrane would disintegrate).
>
> 4. **Confusing "Bake" vs "UV Crosslink"** — Nitrocellulose must be **baked**; nylon is **UV crosslinked**. Reversing these is a frequent error.
>
> 5. **Mixing up the Order of Hybridization and Washing** — Pre-hybridization → Hybridization → Washing. Pre-hybridization is **NOT** the same as washing.
>
> 6. **Wrong Random Primer Description** — Examiners expect "**random hexanucleotide primers + Klenow fragment**" — not just "random primers." The hexamer length is mandatory.
>
> 7. **Ignoring Formamide in Tm Calculation** — A common error that leads to a 30 °C discrepancy in Tm and wrong hybridization temperature recommendation.
>
> 8. **Not Mentioning the Denhardt's Solution Components** — When describing pre-hybridization, the three components (Ficoll, BSA, PVP) are often forgotten. Examiners look for this in 7-mark questions.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY LAST-MINUTE REVISION SHEET — MEMORIZE BEFORE EXAM**

- ✅ **Inventor**: Edwin M. Southern, **1975**, University of Edinburgh.
- ✅ **Purpose**: Detect a specific **DNA** sequence among many (gene identification, RFLP, GMO confirmation).
- ✅ **Six Core Steps**: Digest → Electrophorese → Denature → Blot → Fix → Hybridize → Wash → Detect.
- ✅ **Restriction enzymes** used: *Eco*RI, *Hin*dIII, *Bam*HI, etc.
- ✅ **Gel concentration**: 0.7–1.0% agarose in **1× TAE or TBE** buffer.
- ✅ **Denaturation solution**: 1.5 M NaCl + 0.5 M NaOH (NOT boiling).
- ✅ **Neutralization solution**: 1 M Tris-HCl (pH 7.4) + 1.5 M NaCl.
- ✅ **Transfer buffer**: **20× SSC** (3 M NaCl, 0.3 M sodium citrate, pH 7.0).
- ✅ **Transfer time**: **12–18 hours** by capillary action.
- ✅ **Membrane**: Nitrocellulose (bake 80 °C, 2 h) or Nylon (UV crosslink **120 mJ/cm²**).
- ✅ **Pre-hybridization**: 6× SSC + **5× Denhardt's** (Ficoll, BSA, PVP) + 0.5% SDS + denatured salmon sperm DNA.
- ✅ **Hybridization temperature**: **T_m − 15 to 25 °C** (with formamide: T_m − 15 °C).
- ✅ **High stringency wash**: **0.1× SSC, 0.1% SDS, 65 °C**.
- ✅ **Three major probe labeling methods**: **Random priming, Nick translation, End-labeling (T4 PNK)**.
- ✅ **Random priming reagents**: Random **hexanucleotide primers** + Klenow fragment + labeled dNTPs.
- ✅ **Detection systems**: Autoradiography (³²P), Chemiluminescence (DIG-CSPD), Colorimetry (BCIP/NBT).
- ✅ **Tm formula**: $T_m = 81.5 + 16.6 \log_{10}[\text{Na}^+] + 0.41(\%GC) - 600/N - 0.61(\% \text{ formamide})$.
- ✅ **Formamide effect**: Each 1% lowers Tm by **0.61 °C**.
- ✅ **Mismatch effect**: Each 1% mismatch lowers Tm by **~1.4 °C**.
- ✅ **Wallace rule (for ≤14 nt oligos)**: $T_m = 2(A+T) + 4(G+C)$ — DO NOT use for long probes.
- ✅ **Naming convention**: Southern = DNA (inventor), Northern = RNA (joke), Western = Protein (joke).
- ✅ **Positive control**: Plasmid with confirmed cloned insert digested with same enzyme.
- ✅ **Negative control**: Empty vector digested with same enzyme (no insert band expected).
- ✅ **Membrane choice for KTU lab**: Nylon preferred (better binding, re-probable, durable).
